bibl.author: Wilks, Thomas Egerton
bibl.title: The King's Wager; or, The Camp, The Cottage, and the Court
A Drama in three acts
Lacy's Acting Edition, volume 62, No. 0929

event.desc: Premiered at Royal Victoria Theatre 11 Dec. 1837; Nicoll date 1837-12-05

revisionDesc.change: Add wikidata link to titleStmt where possible
Much hand editing, checked against UM copy

titlePage.titlePart: or, The Camp, the Cottage, and the Court.
titlePage.byline: BY THOMAS EGERTON WILKS, author of Lord Darnley;Eily O'Connor; The Seven Clerks; The Black Domino; My Wife's Dentist; Sudden Thoughts; The Brothers; 'Tis She, State Secrets, My Valet and I Bamboozling, &c. &c.

performance.p: As performed at the Royal Victoria Theatre, on Monday, December 11, 1837, (under the management of Mr. Oxberry), THE KING’S WAGER; OR, THE CAMP, THE COTTAGE, AND THE COURT.

The Music composed and arranged by Mr. LEIGH SMITH. The New and Splendid Scenery including Views of Windsor and Eton (as they formerly appeared) from rare and authentic prints, by Mr. JAMES and Assistants. The Machinery by Mr. MOULDS. The Dresses by Miss PHAROAH. The Properties by Mr. MACDONALD.

castItem.roleDesc: (a Wanderer from Worcester Fight, striving to escape to Normandy)
(an Officer of the Army of Parliament, and Favourite of Cromwell)

performance.p: Between the First and Second Acts a period of 12 Years is presumed to elapse.

castItem.roleDesc: (King of England, sojourning at Windsor Castle)

performance.p: Courtiers, Pages of Honour, Lords and Ladies, Watchmen, Ruffians, &c., by a numerous Train of Auxiliaries, engaged to appear in this Drama.

list.head: Costumes for the First Part.
list.item: CHARLES THE SECOND.—Dark doublet, full black velvet breeches, knees trimmed with lace, high russet boots, disguise cloak, and black slouched hat.
SEDLEY.—Brown doublet and breeches, high russet boots, disguise cloak, and slouched hat.
CLAYFORD.—Red doublet, cloak, fall trunks, trimmed with blue silk and buttons, and high russet boots.
TATTERSHALL.—Dark blue Guernsey shirt, black glazed hat, petticoat trousers, and boots.
COLONEL VANE.—Grey doublet and trunks, steel gorget, black hat, high buff boots, cross belt and pistols and gauntlets.
SMALLTAP.—Blue doublet, short cloak, trunks to knees, with red tape and black braid, black hose, and russet shoes and rosettes.
OYASIUS.—Slate coloured doublet, dark buttoned breehes, russet boots, and black slouched hat.
SERJEANT.—Buff leather jerkin, breast-plate, drab breeches, high russet boots, belt, and sword, helmet.

CORPORAL PETER.—Drab serge jerkin, plain armour breast plate back, buff cross belt and basket hilt sword, black leather waist-belt with brass buckle, plain iron helmet, large square cut puritanical linen collar, treoper’s slouch russets boots, hair cut close, no moustache or whisker.
SOLDIERS OF THE COMMONWEALTH.—Red doublets, armour breast-plates, and helmets.
ROSABELLE—Plain doublets and breeches, large disguise cloak, slouch hat, and russet boots.
EUNICE.—Straw coloured petticoat, black velvet hoddice.

list.head: Costumes for the Second Part.
list.item: CHARLES.—Purple velvet embroidered body, with short sleeves, lace under-sleeves, purple velvet skirt, white tights, purple sash garters, purple-shoes, sword, drab beaver hat and purple feather. (For example of this style of dress, see see. Lacy’s Dramatic Costumes, plate 125).
CLARENDON.—Black velvet dress, like the above. (see Lacy’s Dramatic Costumes, plate 49)
ORMOND, SEDLEY, WALLER, BUCKINGHAM, and NOBLES.—Dresses similar to the above varying in colour.
LOVELL and JOYCE.—Light satin dresses embroidered, of the above fashion.
VANE.—Dark jerkin, trunk breeches, gray tights, boots, slouch hat.
MIRONDELLE.—Full red cloth sack coat, blue trunk breeches, red stockings, broad brimmed hat and feather, broad belt and sword, shoes.
PETER.—Doublet, trunk breeches, red stockings and shoes. TYBBE.—Light grey doublet, full trunks, small cloak, stockings and shoes.
WATCHMEN.—Full overcoats, night caps, broad brimmed hats, horn lanterns, and bills.
MESSENGER.—Coat, trunk breeches, boots and spurs, hat and feather.
RUFFIANS.—Slouch hats, doublets, and trunk breeches, boots, swords and daggers, disguise cloaks.

DUCHESS OF DEVONSHIRE AND LADIES OF RANK.—Open satin dresses, trimmed with lace and jewels, small curls over brow and full curls, dressed with pearls and jewels, fans, &c. If small spaniels of the King Charles breed can be obtained, the ladies might each carry one. (see Lacy’s Costumes, plates 11, 52, 114, and others.)
ROSABELLE.—Plain grey dress of the time, white petticoat. FLORA.—A waiting maid of the time (see Lacy’s Costumes, plate 16.)

body.note: This Drama is divided into Two Parts, each of which conmstitutes a perfect play, and they can be performed singly as well as together

div.head: THE KING’S WAGER; OR, THE CAMP, THE COURT, AND THE COTTAGE.
PART I.—THE CAMP.—1651.

SCENE FIRST.—The Travellers Rest Hostelrie, R. U. E., with signboard, “The Travellers’ Rest, kept by Master Small-tap Watch-and-learn” –at back the sea is seen as from an elevation overhanging it; ship at anchor.
div.stage: Music.–
" Enter LASSES, R. and L., who welcome SAILORS, who enter from under the stage, L. as coming up from the beach.

sp.speaker: Chorus.
sp.l: Hail to the shores of our native land,
Where all are brave and free:
Where warm hearts glow, on cliffs of snow,

div.stage: The GIRLS and all the SAILORS exeunt, R.
"Enter Tattershall and two Sailors up rocks at C.

sp.speaker: Tattershall
sp.p: (R.) Well, lads, here we are, once more in England. England is not now the merry England it was once, but let us hope that better times are in store for her.

sp.speaker: 1st Sailor
sp.p: (L.) Aye, aye, when Charley gets his own again—Why, then—

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: Hush! These are dangerous words, for just now the cause of the king is more like a wreck than anything else. Hows’ever, let’s have some grog. (goes towards, house R. U. E. and reads sign) Ah! what’s this? “The Travellers’ Rest, kept by Master Smalltap Watch-and-learn.” Hey! why what is become of the old landlord, Master Humphrey? I must enquire. (taps at door)

div.stage: Enter Smalltap from door R. U. E.

sp.speaker: Smalltap
sp.p:
What’s wanted?—only say! I’m the man:
I mean the landlord! (aside) They’re a very suspicious looking set...

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: What has become of old Humphrey, who formerly kept this house?

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Gone—gone to sleep!

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: Dead? That’s unlucky! The cause has lost a good supporter in him.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: (aside) The cause? That’s suspicious! What cause?

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: What cause? Why, the cause of the king to be sure.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: The king? that’s remarkably suspicious! Oh, we know nothing about kings here, we don’t deal in the article. I almost begin to think that you are one of Charles Stuart’s men.

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: Why, look’ye, sir, if I see a poor fellow, no matter whether he be king or commoner, running away from his enemies, who mean, if they catch him, to kill him without any fault upon his part, and I can give him a lift out of the danger, I’m not the man to say no!

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: That’s suspicious! Do you know where Charles Stuart is now?

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: Not exactly, but I suppose he’s sailing somewhere about here, I want to get him aboard my vessel, and—whew! I must be cautious—I say if it lay in your power to do so, wouldn’t you assist him to escape ?

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: There’s a thousand pounds offered for his head!

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: Well, and what of that?

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Why, it’s a large sum of money !.

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: Why, Smalltap, you don’t mean as how the money would be any temptation to you to betray him—do you?

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: It might be to some. (aside) It would to me!

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: Might it? Well, now I’ll tell you what I think of that—if one man betrays another for the sake of gold, he ought to be strung up at the yard-arm for it, and damme, (shows pistol) if I’d mind shooting him myself!

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: (aside) This man is a most suspicious character. I shall keep at a profound distance from him.

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: But come, lads, and let’s taste the liquor here, for I hold one glass of comfortable grog to be worth, at least, three hours’ argument.

div.stage: Music.— Tattershall and Sailors exeunt into house, L. U. E. Smalltap watches them suspiciously.
Enter Oyasius, L. U. E.

sp.speaker: Oyasius
sp.p: Hold! I would speak with you.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Who are you ?

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: I am a friend to the Commonwealth; I am a hater of monarchy. I would do my country a service, and make my own fortune by the act.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Give me your hand; you’re a man after my own heart.

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: Yes; and in the scheme by which I purpose effecting my ends, I mean to make you a partaker. Listen—in a short time, that is, between this hour and twilight, Charles Stuart will be in yonder house.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Charles Stuart?

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: Even so; that very house. Lo! from Boscabel have I tracked his footsteps hitherward, like unto the hunter watching the prey he designs to ensnare; now he is within my power; aid me to make him prisoner, and half the rich reward shall be thine own.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Half?—Five hundred pounds? Only tell me what’s to be done?

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: Observe me—Charles, attended by one friend, will be here almost immediately, not knowing that old Humphrey, who formerly kept this house, and who was a firm loyalist, is dead, they intend resting in your hostelrie for a few hours, and then embarking in yonder vessel for Normandy.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: I’ll alarm the household –I’ll publish the affair to the world—here—hallo!

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: Hold! Not a soul must know of it.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Not a soul, nor a body either?

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: No, we must keep our own counsel, else the reward will be divided amongst too many.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: That’s true, I’ll be mum. How are we to take them?

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: They are but two, and we are two.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: No, we’re not; we are only one—I don’t fight—I only pray!

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: But to make certain of the game, I purpose going instantly to the camp of General Fairfax, and there procuring soldiers, then return and make our prey safe prisoners.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: That’s a thousand times better plan—soldiers were made for fighting.

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: In the mean time do you watch carefully, and see they do not leave the house.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: I’ll take care of that.

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: Do you know the man, Charles Stuart ?

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Not by sight, because I never saw him.

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: You may easily recognize him, for he will tell you that he is an officer in the army of Parliament, and wishes to be secret here, and likewise that he only designs staying in your house till daybreak. By all this you may know him.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Oh, I’ll find him out I’ll warrant. I haven’t called myself—Master “Watch-and-learn,” for nothing. I say, I’m to have half the reward?

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: Yes!

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Good! That’s just five hundred pounds.

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: Be careful. I will return an hour ere daybreak. Now for the camp. (crosses to R.) Charles Stuart once in my power, this simple-minded innkeeper may look in vain for his share of the reward; that must be mine own. Exit, R.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: I’ll see if I can’t take Charles Stuart prisoner before that fellow comes back, and then he may whistle for his share of the reward. One thousand pounds are a vast deal better than five hundred. I’ll keep a keen look out in this affair, and when Charles Stuart does come he’ll not only find that he’s suspected, but expected; but I must manage the whole reward—five from five and nothing remains, but five and five are ten. Oh, I must have it all! Music. Exit into house.

div.stage: Enter Vane, supporting Rosabelle, L. U. E. (disguised in male attire.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: Courage, courage, my Rosabelle, we have not far to go—here is the house of entertainment, we can here seek welcome rest, and when the dawn breaks pursue our journey.

sp.speaker: Rosabelle
sp.p: (L.) Alas, dear Vane, I am greatly fatigued.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: I know thou must be so; confusion on the blockhead who undertook to meet us with fresh horses, and then forgot his promise; but ’tis only a short distance now to Brighthelmstone, and once there we are safe.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Should my father discover our elopement—follow and overtake us, ere I become thy bride, we are parted for ever.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: I know the stake at hazard well, and will lose it only with my life; when I persuaded thee, Rosabelle, to fly with me from thy father’s house, ’twas only the certainty that he would never consent to our nuptials, that induced me to propose an act of disobedience; I know that he loves you, and when his first anger is over, he will forgive his darling child.

div.stage: Smalltap appears at door, R.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Hallo! there they are, sure enough. That’s the king, as the sailor calls him, and that’s his friend; but which is Charles Stuart? I should like to know that.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: Come, let us to the house. Oh, good friend, are you not host here?

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Yes, good friend, I am; pray, who are you?

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: I am an officer of the army of Parliament.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: It’s him! I feel as though I had got the thousand pounds already.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: We seek shelter in your house till daybreak.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Till daybreak? It’s all right! You shall have it, and welcome too!

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: We would be very private?

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: I dare say!

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: And if we are enquired for, you will act wisely if you say we passed not this way; you understand.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Oh, yes, I understand. It’s him!

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: To-night then you will provide for us.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Yes, I’ll provide for you to-night. (aside) And to-morrow you’ll be provided for at the expense of the nation.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: Come dearest! Exit with Rosabelle into the house, R. U. E.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: That’s him! that’s him! but which is him? Why, I think it must be the little one, because he tried to hide his face, yes, yes, it’s the little one, and yet, now I think on’t, it may be the tall one, for it was him that said he was an officer; oh, yes, it’s the tall one, and yet, it may be the little one, for he looked more like a cavalier, having the longest hair. Yes, yes, it’s the little one—no, no, it’s the tall one—it may be the little one– I know it’s the tall one—I mean I know it’s the little one. I know it’s the– I’ll be shot if I know which it is. Music.—Exit into house, R. U. E.

div.stage: Enter Sedley cautiously L. U. E., looks round, and then beckons on Charles, L. U. E. The stage gradually darkens till the close of the scene, when it has become twilight.

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: (R.) At length we have reached the coast in safety, and here is the house to which Tattershall directed us, and yet he said the landlord’s name was Humphrey, did he not, my liege?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: In truth I know not, Master Richard, my memory is a most unaccommodating one, for all those things which I ought to remember I forget; and all those things which I ought to forget I remember. For instance, I can recollect well how I relished last night’s supper of cold beef, concealed under a truss of straw in a stable, but cannot for the life o’ me remember the name of the person who gave it.

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: Was not this Humphrey formerly a soldier, sire?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: He was, I remember him well; he was then a rude, ranting roysterer, but now seeks the true path in a sad coloured doublet, and a closely-cut head of hair.

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: We must be cautious not to discover ourselves until we have ascertained whether the new host may be safely trusted. What, ho! house here!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Yes, yes, we must be cautious, decidedly cautious, for our loving subjects have a strong desire to cut off this head of ours, the which, we, ourselves, prefer keeping in its present situation. Shout again, sir, and if a pretty girl should answer the call, why—

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: Hush! my liege, I pray you. House!

div.stage: Enter Eunice, door R.U.E.

sp.speaker: Eunice
sp.p: (R.) Did you call, sir?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: (L.) No, my dear, he hooted.

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: (C.) Pray, my dear, does your father keep this house?

sp.speaker: Eunice
sp.p: He did, sir; but he has long been dead.

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: That is unlucky!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Never mind, he has left his daughter behind.

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: Who is master here now? Is his name the same as on that signboard?

sp.speaker: Eunice
sp.p: His name? Oh, heaven! what can they want? In these sad times the presence of every stranger excites surprise and terror.

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: Speak, wench; his name, I say?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Silence, Richard, you are too stern; here, see how I’ll manage matters. (crosses to c.) Tell me, my pretty girl, who is landlord here?

sp.speaker: Eunice
sp.p: To be sure, noble sir; my master is landlord, his name is Smalltap Watch-and-learn.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: There, Richard, you see what a little management does; and now, my beauty, you must give me one kiss of these ruby lips.

sp.speaker: Eunice
sp.p: Oh dear, no, sir; my master would stop it out of my wages.

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: (L.) Pray, my lord, be more prudent

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: I’ll have a kiss, I swear (struggles with and kisses Eunice.)

div.stage: Enter Smalltap, door B. U. E.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Hallo! what’s this I see? A strange man kissing my maid? That’s suspicious! I’m afraid he’s no better than he should be! Why, Eunice ––(coming down, R.)

sp.speaker: Eunice
sp.p: (R.) Oh, goodness! my master!

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Get into the house, you huzzy! What do you think the Reverend Mr. Fast-and-pray Fireshovel would say if he saw such goings on? Kissed by a man! A man who has done nothing for the house too, hasn’t spent a penny in it yet. Oh, shameful! shameful! Exit Eunice, door R. U. E.

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: Pray, sire, be more cautious.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p:
(C.) Never heed it; if Rochester has kept his
word, and we the road, to-morrow evening we shall sleep in Normandy.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: (R.) And now, sir, what have you got to say about kissing my maid?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Kissing your maid? Why, did I do so ?

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Why, if you did not do so, it was one of the best imitations I ever saw.

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: Pray, do you keep this house?

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: No, no, the house keeps me; who are you that ask?

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: We are officers of the army of the Parliament.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Hey?—What? Why?—My good gracious!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: And we would stay in your house till daybreak.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Till daybreak! Why, bless me! perhaps these are the men instead of t’others, and t’others are these; now I think of it, his kissing Eunice, was particularly suspicious?

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: Can you accommodate us?

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Oh, dear yes! This way gentles. (they cross to R.) But I say don’t you kiss Eunice any more?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Oh, no, no, no, not till the next time, for certain.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Because, if you do, I shall charge it in the bill, for certain.

div.stage: Sedley and Charles exit door R. U. E.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: I’m completely bothered, whether this is the king, or whether the other’s the king; or, whether the other’s king, or whether this is the king—one thing is quite certain, they can’t all four be kings. I must mind what I’m about here, or I shall lose the money. Oh, I only wish I knew which was the king. Exit door, R. U. E.

div.head: Scene Second.–
div.stage: Interior of Tent in the Camp of General Fairfax, centre open, with canvas curtains, C., backed by a Wood.
Enter Sergeant and eight Soldiers, with drinking cups, and Peter Praying-for-peace, with a canteen slung round his neck, R.—Stage light.

sp.speaker: Sergeant
sp.p: Silence! I say, silence! Hear me, my brethen, while I expound to you the axioms of the Reverend Master Barebones.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: (drunk, R.) That’s right, I like expounds, and compounds, as well. (drinks)

sp.speaker: Sergt.
sp.p: When our Colonel is here, we must be sober, ergo we don’t drink; when our Colonel is not here, we needn’t be sober, ergo ––

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: Ergo, we get drunk, and a very pretty, innocent amusement drinking is. It gives one such celestial ideas. (drinks a knock, L. C.)

sp.speaker: Sergt.
sp.p:
Hallo! who can that be? If the Colonel does
come to-night—but surely, it cannot be he. Hide your cups, lads, hide your cups. (they do so)

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: If we do hide our cups, we shall hide ourselves; for we are in our cups, and if the Colonel finds us in our cups, he’ll hide us.

sp.speaker: Sergt.
sp.p: Ha! who are these men approaching? (looks, L. C.) Exit 1st Soldier, L. C.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: No one could tell now that we were Colonel Vane’s men, we are all disguised—disguised in liquor.

sp.speaker: Sergt.
sp.p: Corporal Peter Praying-for-peace, you keep as much out of sight as possible, you are the most drunken of us all.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: Calls me drunk; that’s an insult it’s an insult to say I’m drunk, and when I’m sober I’ll resent it. (drinks)

sp.speaker: Sergt.
sp.p: Stand back, I say.

div.stage: Enter 1st Soldier, Clayford, and two Attendants, C.

sp.speaker: Clayford
sp.p: L. C. Where is the Colonel? Where is he, who, in defiance of all laws, divine or human, hath stolen away my child?

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: The old gentleman’s tipsy.

sp.speaker: Sergt.
sp.p: The Colonel is not here.

sp.speaker: Clay.
sp.p: Not here? I must have passed him on the road. I’ll instantly retrace my steps, and search every hovel wherein it is possible that he and this deluded girl may have sought shelter; and, if – if I do meet him, he shall find that though my arm is old, it still is strong enough to punish villany.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: He’s roaring drunk; it’s disgraceful to see such a an old man in such a state. (drinks)

sp.speaker: Clay.
sp.p: Follow me, lads, the darkness of the night shall not detain us. Herbert Vane, look to yourself! Exeunt with two Attendants, L. to L. C.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: Well, he’s been having a regular drinking bout, however.

sp.speaker: Sergt.
sp.p: (looking, L. C.) Another coming—who is he?

sp.speaker: 1st Soldier
sp.p: (looking, L. C.) It’s the pious Master Oyasius Proudflesh.

sp.speaker: Sergt.
sp.p: Indeed! what can he want with us? Something important doubtless, for he is not given to vain words—his are goodly maxims; they strengthen and comfort my inward man.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: Just what the brandy does for me.

sp.speaker: Sergt.
sp.p: Stand back, Corporal; Master Proudflesh is a keen observer, and will detect the state you are in.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: If he does, tell him I’m in a religious transport!

div.stage: Enter Oyasius Proudflesh, C. from L.

sp.speaker: Oyasius
sp.p:
(L.) Sergeant! I must have your assistance, and
that of your men immediately; in less than five hours Charles Stuart will be your prisoner!

sp.speaker: Sergt.
sp.p: Is it possible?

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: Possible? no doubt! You see this fellow is as tipsy as the other was?

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: ’Tis possible, and true; at the “Traveller’s Rest” on the top of the cliff, lies he concealed.

sp.speaker: Sergt.
sp.p: You may command our aid.

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: Follow me, then. In a cause like this, all our hearts should join, for the man is our country’s foe. Give me a weapon, I’ll prove that those who preach know how, likewise when occasion calls, to strike. Sergeant gives him a pistol Sooner than see him escape, this hand shall consign him to the grave. Follow me, quickly, and the prize is ours! Exit, L. C.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: I say, Sergeant, isn’t that fellow precious drunk?

sp.speaker: Sergt.
sp.p: Drunk?—Nonsense, ’tis you, sir, are drunk.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: That’s an insult. I’ll wash it down. (drinks)

sp.speaker: Sergt.
sp.p: Four of you men must accompany me—Hezekiel Humble-the-foe, Goliah Great-in-fight, Daniel Drinkwater, and Simon Preach-the-truth. (each man takes a step forward as he is named)

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: And am I to go?

sp.speaker: Sergt.
sp.p: Yes; we shall start in a very little time, and so, I would advise you to humble the spirit in private for a few minutes before we go.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: If I humble any spirit it will be the spirit of brandy.

sp.speaker: Sergt.
sp.p: Learn to be more abstemious, Peter; brandy is your greatest enemy, and therefore you ought to hate it.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: No such thing; brandy is my greatest enemy, but then I ought to love it; because we are told to love our enemies, you know. (hugs canteen) Oh, you dear creature!

div.stage: Exeunt Sergeant, the Soldiers, and Peter last, drinking, R.

div.head: SCENE THIRD.—
div.stage: Hall of the Traveller’s Rest; doors, R. and L., in flat; door with bar, L. U. E.; window practicable, R. U. E.; table with bottle, two drinking cans, two plates, refreshments, lighted lamp, and two chairs, R.; table with two bottles, three cans, two plates, refreshments, lighted lamp, and two chairs, L.
Charles and Sedley seated L.—Vane and Rosabelle seated R.—Smalltap, watching, centre—Eunice attending L. table.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: I haven’t found out which is the king yet; I never was so puzzled before.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Come, mine host, here is wine; sit down, and be merry.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Merry! that’s suspicious everybody knows that Charles is fond of being merry.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: This is but poor accommodation for an officer of the army of the Parliament.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: An officer of the army of Parliament—that’s suspicious!

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: Never heed; after the hardships I have endured, the rudest resting place is welcome.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: That’s the king! he’s alluding to his escape from Worcester.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: That’s a very nice girl, master landlord.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: That’s the king! Charles is fond of the petticoats.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: I must scrape acquaintance with her.

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: Be prudent, I pray.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: The other is telling him to be prudent—that’s the king.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: Host, have you a separate apartment for my companion?

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: A separate apartment? He doesn’t like sleeping with his attendant—that’s the king. Your majest—I—hem! —I mean, sir, no, I have only two rooms to let there is one. (points to R—Charles who has been playing with Eunice, now kisses her—Smalltap turns round) Hallo! he’s got hold of Eunice again—that’s the king!

sp.speaker: Eunice
sp.p: Be quiet, sir, can’t you?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: (rising) Not while you are here:

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Then you’d better go away. Exit Eunice, L. U. E. That’s the king!

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: (rising) Come, host, show us to our chamber, we must be moving at daybreak.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Wants to move off, no doubt.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: (rising) And we, too, must seek rest, for ’ere the day breaks, we must be gone.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Well, now, this is odd—odd! no, it’s even, for there’s four of them, four fugitives all wanting to run away at daybreak. Well, friend, that’s your chamber. (to Vane, and points to R. flat) And that, gentles, is yours. (to Charles, and points to L. flat)

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: (aside to him) Come, your majesty, let us retire.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Majesty! Alack, I look but little like majesty. Marry, the knave who said that majesty, deprived of its external, was nothing save “a jest,” was marvellously near the truth. I find the jest a sorry one; no matter, ’tis useless indulging such reflections.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p:
Come, dearest Rosabelle, you must seek slumber’s reviving power, whilst I, at your chamber door, watch that no harm approach you. (takes lamp, Sedley takes another, all say
“Good night.”-Vane and Rosabelle exeunt, R. flat—Sedley and Charles at L. flat—stage half dark)

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Well, I’ll be hanged if I know which is the king now! What’s to be done? I can’t help thinking that the tall chap who was flirting with Eunice, must be the king. I’ll make sure of him—I’ll settle his business! (locks L. door flat) Now I’ve got him safe—now I shall get my thousand pounds—now I shall get my and yet, if it should happen that it’s one of the others! Egad, I know what I’ll do—I’ll make all sure. (locks R. door flat) Now I’m sure I’ve got him, and now I’ve got him the next thing is, what shall I do with him? I’ll go and turn that over in my mind, while I take a turn in the garden. I’ve turned the key on them—one good turn deserves another. Takes lamp and exits, R. U. E.—stage quite dark.

div.stage: Music. Tattershall enters cautiously through window.

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: Dark as a night watch! Can’t see a step before me, but that can’t be helped. The king is in peril, and everything must be attempted to rescue him. Soldiers are now on their way from Brighthelmstone to seize him. No time must be lost. I know he’s here, for I saw him whilst I was watching through the casement. He’s gone now to his hammock. (feeling about, gets to door R.C. flat) Yes, yes, and this is the door of his cabin; gone to sleep may be Hist! hist! hillo!

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: (within R.) Who calls?

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: A friend! Open the door.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: (within) By heavens! ’tis locked.

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: Oh, damme, then break it (a crash is heard, door opens)

div.stage: Enter Vane, R. C.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: Who are you?

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: Your firm friend; your enemies are in pursuit, and will be here almost directly.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: (aside) Ha! then the implacable father of Rosabelle is determined to tear her from me; but no, I will perish first.

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: I will assist you to escape!

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: Generous friend!

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: Bring your companion, and I will conduct you by a short and easy path to the coast.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: A thousand thanks! (going to door) Rosabelle!

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: (L.) ’Tis fortunate I arrived in time to save the king!

div.stage: Enter Rosabelle, door, R. C.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p:
Rosabelle, your father still pursues us, and is close
at hand. Had it not been for yonder stranger, we should have at once been separated; but he has kindly offered to lead us to a place of safe concealment.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Oh, let us then hasten onward; I dare not meet my father’s angry eye.

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: Now, your honour, I know who you are, but I shall not mention your name, for fear somebody else may hear; this way. (crosses to R.) I have no light, but I know every step of ground about here, many a keg of brandy (at window R.) have I dragged up this cliff—this way. Music.— They cautiously get through window. Loud knock, L. U. E.

div.stage: Enter Smalltap, with lamp, R. U. E.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Hollo! there are the soldiers, now for the prize. (unbars the door, L. U. E.) Enter Clayford and two attendants, L. U. E. A Stranger!

sp.speaker: Clay.
sp.p: You have two fugitives here?

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Hollo! another got hold of the secret? My five hundred pounds are melting down into two hundred and fifty, I’ll play a bold game—No, I have not!

sp.speaker: Clay.
sp.p: Villain, ’tis false, I read it in your eye.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Which eye?

sp.speaker: Clay.
sp.p: Beware! beware how you give them shelter!

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Hollo! I’m getting into a mess! If they think I’m sheltering Charles Stuart, I shall get hanged! I must be content with my two hundred and fifty. Well, sir, I’ll speak the truth, I have got two fugitives here, and as I thought they seemed suspicious characters, I put them under lock and key. (shews key)

sp.speaker: Clay.
sp.p: You acted rightly, and shall be rewarded,

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Rewarded! of course, I shall expect two hundred and fifty pounds.

sp.speaker: Clay.
sp.p: Produce them!

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: (unlocks L. door) Here, you’re wanted; come out.

div.stage: Enter Charles and Sedley, door, L. C.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: (covering his face with cloak) Great heavens! I fear we are betrayed!

sp.speaker: Clay.
sp.p: (R.) Ha! even through that disguise, well contrived as it is, I know you.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: It’s all right! it’s him sure enough!

sp.speaker: Clay.
sp.p: (seizes Charles’s arm) Perfidious, deceitful girl!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: L.C. Girl?

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Girl? What a whopper!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: (bursting from Clayford) Release me, sir; what does this mean?

sp.speaker: Clay.
sp.p: That voice! you cannot be my daughter!

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: His daughter? The old fellow’s bewitched.

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: There’s some mistake here!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: And a very lucky one it is for us. (drops cloak) My lord of Clayford, your loyalty is known to me. I am Charles Stuart! Clayford and two Attendants kneel

sp.speaker: Clay.
sp.p: The king!

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: It’s him!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: I am in peril, my lord !

sp.speaker: Clay.
sp.p: Sire, command me!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: What seek you here?

sp.speaker: Clay.
sp.p: I am in pursuit of a recreant daughter, who has fled from me, with a rebel to your Majesty’s crown.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Are your men here to be trusted?

sp.speaker: Clay.
sp.p: I will answer for them with my life !

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: ’Tis well; now observe, while Sedley and myself descend to the sands, do you with your attendants guard the summit of the path; once on the shore, I am safe, for there waits a boat to bear me from this coast of danger.

sp.speaker: Clay.
sp.p: Sire, it shall be done.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Enough; come, Sedley, follow me.

div.stage: Music—Exeunt all but Smalltap, door, L. U. E.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Why, here’s my five hundred pounds melting away into nothing; all my money floating away from me, and I not able to help myself. What’s the reason that fool of a fellow doesn’t return with the soldiers? Talk of dividing the money with me indeed! why, we shall have no money to divide.

div.stage: Music—Oyasius rushes on, door, L. U. E., with pistol.

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: Villain! who were the men, that just now left this house? Speak quickly, or –

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Don’t be rash! move that ugly weapon away; it may go off unawares, and then I shall lose my life as well as my money—one of them was Charles Stuart. Where are your men?

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: Some wait without, others I have despatched to the beach.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: To the beach? There’s life in a muscle –we’ll have him yet. Oh, my five hundred pounds! I’m all alive again! Come along—I know a near way to the sands—come along— Oh, my five hundred pounds! Exit, door, L. U. E.

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: (looking at priming of pistol) Now, Fortune, uncertain and fickle as thou art, do but befriend me in this one matter, and ever more I will deride thy frown. Music—Exit door, L. U. E.

div.head: Scene Fourth.—
div.stage: View in the Vicinity of Shoreham (1st grooves)
Enter Tattershall, Vane, and Rosabelle, L.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: ’Tis a rough road you are leading us.

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: True; but ’tis a near path and little frequented; we shall be upon the sands almost immediately, and when there, you may defy pursuit.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: Thanks, thanks, for thy kindness! Exit Tattershall, R.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Whither is that man leading us ?

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: To the coast, dearest; be not alarmed, although his manner is rough, ’tis honest, and I feel that we may safely trust him. Music—Exeunt with Rosabelle, R.

div.stage: Enter Sergeant with four Soldiers, Oyasius, and Smalltap, L.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: There they go, we shall have them directly—Oh, my five hundred pounds!

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: Now, lads, keep close to me, and the moment yonder fugitives—reach the sands make them your prisoners. If they attempt to escape, shoot them.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: No, no, I won’t have any shooting, no bloodshed. I’ve got religious scruples on that point.

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: Pish! pshaw!

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: But a thought strikes me, Master Proudflesh—will the government give as much for Charles Stuart dead, as for Charles Stuart alive?

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: Precisely the same.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Then fire away; my scruples are gone.

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: Forward, lads, stand by me now; and when the prize is taken, I’ll not forget the men who aided in the chase !

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Yes, go on; let’s have the business over, and the money safe; but as it’s likely there will be some shooting, why I’ll go last, for fear I should rob you of any portion of the honour and renown. Music—Exeunt, R.

div.head: Scene Fifth. —
div.stage: The Sussex coast, close to the town of Shoreham. (Daybreak.)—Rocky path, L.U.E.— Vessel at anchor.
Peter and four Soldiers discovered.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p:
It’s only persons that really are soldiers, that know the perils and privations of a soldier’s life. Here was I obliged to leave a comfortable barracks, and march six long miles in the dead of the night, just too as I was going to bed, and all to catch a runaway king; forgot to bring my brandy too, that’s the worst part of the business. I’ve filled it points to
canteen with water, and been drinking occasionally trying to persuade myself that it’s brandy, but it’s all in vain.

sp.speaker: 1st Soldier
sp.p: (who had been looking off, L.U.E.) Corporal!

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: Yes, my lad, have a drop. Soldier shakes his head You won’t, hey? He knows better—he tasted it before— knows it’s water!

sp.speaker: 1st Soldier
sp.p: Corporal, here are three persons descending this steep path, they must be the parties we want?

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: Not a doubt of it, but stay, don’t seize them directly, wait till I give the signal; I should like to know what they mean to do upon reaching the spot. I’ll take a drop of brandy to hearten me. (puts canteen to his lips) Ugh! I’d forgotten the water! Music.—Peter and Soldiers exeunt, R. U. E.

div.stage: Enter Tattershall, Vane, and Rosabelle, L. U. E.

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: It’s all right; we are unobserved– now to summon the boat. Music. — Fires pistol towards ship, a light from ship is seen, and small boat leaves, L. Here, the boat is coming.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: The boat? What mean you?

div.stage: Peter and Soldiers re-enter, L. U. E.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: Seize them! Music.—Two Soldiers march down to Vane, R. and two to L. of Rosabelle.

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: R. C. Great heavens! the king is lost!

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: No such thing, the king is found!

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: (L.) (aside) Oh, Vane, what can this mean?

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: (L.) I cannot even guess; these are my own men, as they shall presently find.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: L. U. E. Here, hollo! I’ve taken them prisoners.

div.stage: Enter Oyasius followed by Sergeant, four Soldiers, and Smalltap, L. U. E., down path.

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: R. C. Ha, is it so? Then my warmest hopes are fulfilled. Which is the king?

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: L. C., touching Vane This this is the king!

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: Peter Praying-for-peace!

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: Hey! that voice! th-a-t face! Oh, lord, I’ve taken my own Colonel prisoner. Here, you fellows, fall back; don’t you see it’s the Colonel?

sp.speaker: Sergt.
sp.p: Why, so it is; soldiers fall back. The four Soldiers range L., and Sergeant looks off, L. U. E. The other four Soldiers are ranged R.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: The Colonel looks angry; I shall go, I’ve made a mistake here. (sneaks off, R.)

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: R. C., a little up And I have made a mistake here.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: (R.) And I’ve made a mistake here, for I thought my five hundred pounds were safe.

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: R. C. Can it be, am I cheated? deceived at last. But your companion, sir, who is he?

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: That’s right; look sharp after him, cos’ of the five hundred!

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: Who my companion is must remain a secret.

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: Ha!

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: That looks suspicious!

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: This is no time for secrecy. I must and will know who your companion is.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Right! quite right! we really must know, I say, my friend! (to Oyasius) Stick to him !

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: Clothed as you appear to be, sir, now with a semblance of authority, I suppose I must obey, however unwillingly. Know then that this person is very dear to me, this person is a girl.

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: A girl?

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: A girl?

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: A girl? Why, I get more like a fool every day, now I can’t tell a man from a woman!

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: (seizing Smalltap) Villain! what has become of Charles Stuart ?

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: I’ll be hanged if I know.

sp.speaker: Sergt.
sp.p: (who has been looking off, L. U. E.) Ah, through the uncertain mist of the morning, methinks I see two persons approaching. Down the cliff—it must be them!

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: Ha! which way?

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: (R.) It is– it is! I’ll swear that’s Charles Stuart, and I’ll swear he’s no girl.

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: Yes, yes, he comes. Colonel Vane, I charge you, by your fidelity to the cause of the people, to assist us in making these men prisoners. Enter Charles and Sedley, L. U. E. Charles Stuart, you are my prisoner!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: (C.) How, betrayed at last?

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: (between Charles and Oyasius, draws sword) Not while I live; the man who harms the king must cross my prostrate body.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Colonel, ’tis useless offering resistance. I thank your loyalty, but will not call for its exhibition when the price to be paid is blood; the fate that cannot be avoided, must be calmly met. Gentlemen, you may murder me as you did my father; but you shall at least find that I can endure death with as much firmness as he did.

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: I am glad to find you in this humour, for the axe and the scaffold awaits your majesty, and form your portion.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Yes, and the five hundred pounds will be my portion.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Oh, Herbert, if you love me, save the king from this dreadful fate. If you would ever call me wife, preserve him now, nor let these butchers sell his sacred life for filthy gold.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Heaven bless the women! they are always ready to plead the cause of the oppressed.

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: Soldiers, seize these men.

sp.speaker: Sergt.
sp.p: (with Soldiers, R.) Not so, we must await the orders of the colonel.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: And those orders you will not have. Mark me, sir, (to Oyasius) I like this not. When Charles Stuart, at the head of a gallant army, faced me in the battle field, I was his foe, but I war not with fallen men; I repeat I like not this morning’s work, it looks too much like thief-catching to jump with my humour.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: Hollo, hollo! what’s this? Oh, my five hundred!

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: Charles Stuart, you are free. The boat destined to bear you from England’s shores is, I perceive, approaching fast. Go, sir, and in your exile ponder over the important truth that Englishmen never will be the slaves of despotism.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Generous man, I thank thee.

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: (to Vane) Is this your fidelity to the Commonwealth, sir?

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: It is, sir. I prove my fidelity to the Commonwealth by not allowing it to disgrace itself by shedding the blood of a fallen foe. Hark ye, sir, (crosses to Oyasius) Cromwell, who is not so blood-thirsty as some would represent him, has given me private instructions in writing, which are here, (shews papers) not to interfere with Charles Stuart if he be quietly leaving England; and although the Commons have thought fit to offer this large reward, it cannot weigh even as a feather against his order, or my own feelings. (boat appears with Sailors, L. U. E.) Behold, sir, the boat is here.

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: I’ll not be baulked yet.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: That’s right—don’t.

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: Are you a good shot with a pistol?

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: (R.) I don’t know I never tried.

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: R. C. Take this –shoot Charles Stuart through the head as he steps into the boat, unless I first succeed with this. (shews dagger)

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: I’ll try.

div.stage: Enter Clayford and two Attendants, L. U. E.

sp.speaker: Clay.
sp.p: L. C. All is safe, your majesty.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: My father!

sp.speaker: Clay.
sp.p: Ha, deceitful, treacherous girl! (seizes her hand) In the solitude of your chamber in Clayford tower, you shall bitterly rue this disobedience.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Hold, sir—explain this mystery.

sp.speaker: Clay.
sp.p: Sire, ’tis told in a few words. This girl, disregarding my commands, has fled from me with yonder renegade—yonder rebellious Roundhead—who—

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Who possesses a heart which ennobles human nature!

sp.speaker: Clay.
sp.p: Sire!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Colonel Vane has just now saved me from the fate which seemed my destiny, and I will not be ungrateful. He must wed your daughter, for he is worthy of her; nay, turn not away—the king commands—Charles Stuart entreats it.

sp.speaker: Clay.
sp.p: Your majesty’s commands to me are law.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Enough! we thank you. Lady, this is your husband, and should fate ever place me on my father’s throne, you shall find you are not forgotten. (joins their hands)

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: Now be swift and sure; shoot him through the head.

sp.speaker: Small.
sp.p: I don’t like the job; but then the money!

sp.speaker: Tatter.
sp.p: (at the boat) Now, sir, the sun is rising, and the boat is ready.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: And I am ready likewise—Farewell my country! Farewell, friends all! If I never see you again, be assured you have the first place in my grateful memory; but if in future years fortune befriends me better, you shall find that Charles Stuart will remember in Whitehall, the friends whom he now leaves on the coast of Sussex—Farewell!

div.stage: Music to the end goes to the boat.

sp.speaker: Oyas.
sp.p: Perish! (aims blow with dagger at Charles—Vane sees and struggles with him) Fire! destroy him!

div.stage: Smalltap fires at Charles—Vane having perceived the design, drags Oyasius between Charles and Smalltap Oyasius receives the shot—dies, C.—Attendants seize Smalltap, who falls on his knees, R.—Charles in boat, waving his cap, with Sedley, Tattershall and Sailors—four Soldiers by R., and four by L. wings— Rosabelle, Clayford and Vane, L.

div.note: An Interval of 12 Years occurs here.

div.stage: Terrace of Windsor Castle, with portion of the Round Tower, as it appeared in 1663 with bird’s-eye view of Eton, and surrounding country; an embattled tower with Norman casement practicable on L. 2 E.
Enter LILAC LOVELL, R. U. E.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Ha! ha! ha! how merry his majesty is pleased to be this evening. This evening! marry! which evening is it that his majesty is not pleased to be merry? He has indeed merited the title which folks bestow on him of the Merry Monarch; and yet people say that before he was made king he suffered greatly, especially during his escape from Worcester; but if that be the case he has managed somehow or other to entirely dismiss the disagreeable remembrance. But I must try and have a few minutes’ conversation with my deary before any one comes to interrupt us. (sings a verse, ad libitum) She’s coming—she’s coming—she’s here. FLORA appears at window, L. 2 E.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: Is that you, Lilac?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Yes, dear, it is; and that’s you, I see: and see is all I can, for the architect who built this old place was determined nobody outside should do more. Oh! Flora—I’m dying for a kiss.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: A kiss! oh, you naughty boy. How dare you talk so.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Oh, I dare do a great deal more than that. The Duke of Buckingham has been kind enough to take me under his own especial care; I’m a pupil of his, and now I know exactly how women ought to be treated.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: If you talk in such a shocking wicked manner, I’ll close the casement, and never speak to you again. If it be known that I ever allowed a man to talk to me so, I should be ruined; you will please to remember there is a great deal of difference between your master the king, and my mistress the queen.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Exactly so; one’s a man, and the other’s a woman.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: That’s not what I mean; what I mean is, that while your master is a confirmed rake—my mistress is so pious, she’s only fit to be a nun.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: A nun! Oh, I don’t think much of that. The Duke of Buckingham says a nun is as like another woman as two peas.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: The Duke of Buckingham is a shocking—dreadful—dissipated man.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p:
Hush! hush! don’t say a word against my tutor.
But now, Flora, to proceed to business; as the duke justly remarks—there’s nothing like coming to the point with a woman; will you throw a rope ladder out of that casement at twelve to-night?

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: Indeed I will not; and now that I see how wicked you have become, I have done with you for ever—farewell. (shuts casement)

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Hollo! why she’s gone! Why, how in the world did I manage this? I’m sure I acted exactly as the duke told me. (produces a small elegant memorandum book) Yes—here it is precisely as he said it; I must try and bring her back: how is that to be done? I must look for the word “offended” —that’s among the “O’s”—Here it is; (reads) “If you have offended a woman, and wish to make up the quarrel, threaten to kill yourself unless she’ll. forgive you but take care not to do it in reality.” That I’m sure I shan’t. But I’ll try what he recommends. (goes under casement) Good bye, Flora—I’m going to kill myself. I’m so vexed that I have offended you that in exactly fifteen minutes I shall stab myself to the heart.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: (opens casement) Oh, Lilac! Lilac! you can’t mean it.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Oh yes, I do mean it; and I shall do it too—unless you forgive me.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: Oh, pray, give up such a shocking thought—and I will—nay do forgive you.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: (aside) The duke was right; what now does he say? (looks at book) “If she’s very much frightened—be firm in your purpose, until you have made good conditions.” Excellent!

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: You won’t kill yourself, dear? will you?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Yes, I will. I’ll kill myself unless you promise to meet me in the moonlight to-night for a walk.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: Oh, I can’t do that; the queen will want me.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Then I’ll kill myself.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: Oh, gracious! somebody’s coming. (closes casement)

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Why, she’s gone without consenting and I’ve got to kill myself.

div.stage: CHRYSTAL JOYCE enters, R. U. E., looks round.

sp.speaker: Chrystal.
sp.p: Alone! Nobody with him.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Here’s Chrystal! He’s always watching me. Well, Chrystal, what do you want?

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p:
(aside) I’m sure he had some love appointment here, and I’m almost certain that it was with my dear Flora. I wish I could find it out. Why, I don’t want
anything particular, Lilac; I merely came hither to take the air.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: I wish he’d take himself off. How to get rid of him. Chrystal, my dear boy, did you ever notice a beautiful yonng maid of honour—named Flora?

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Notice her? I adore her! and I think you do so too.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: I? Oh dear no; I don’t care about her—oh la! (aside) She may be listening. That is not much—ahem !—but as I know you do, Chrystal, I’ll do a good turn for you.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Why—will you?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Yes; but don’t tell anybody. (confidentially) She’s walking alone in the garden, on the other side of the castle. (aside) That’s what the duke calls a “love lie.”

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Well—and what of that?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: What of that? Why, now’s your time to be sure. Run down there—make desperate love to her—and above all things tell her you are going to kill yourself.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Why, Lilac, if I was alone with her I durstn’t open my mouth—I could’nt speak a word—I’m sure I could’nt.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Just the way it was with me, before the Duke of Buckingham became my tutor. Now I know better—now I do know really how to court. Chrystal, my dear fellow, I’ll give you a bit of advice. Run down to the garden as fast as you can; directly you get to her—kiss her.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Kiss her? I could’nt do it.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Oh, nonsense! Nobody knows what he can do till he tries. If she’s very angry, kiss her; if she gets still more angry, kiss her again; and if then she gets seriously affronted, tell her, that unless she forgives you you’ll kill yourself.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Shall I try that?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: To be sure to be sure. There, run along—run along. (hurries CHRYSTAL off, L.) And if you find her my name is’nt Lilac Lovell. No, no—now that I’m a pupil of the Duke of Buckingham, I mean to keep all the pretty maids of honour to myself. Flora! Flora! I’m a going to kill myself.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: (opens casement) Well, Lilac, this time you have conquered me—I will meet you this evening—but never again.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: A thousand thanks.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: But there is one thing I must say—oh dear, somebody else coming. (shuts casement)

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: How provoking it is to have one’s assignations broken upon in this manner—just as she was going to say something loving too—who is it coming? Why, my tutor, I declare, the Duke himself!

div.stage: Enter BUCKINGHAM, R., with a cane.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Give you good even, my lord!

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Give you good even again, most worthy pupil. How have you fared since we met last?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Oh, capitally, thanks to the instructions I receive from your lordship.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Why, marry, it must be acknowledged you have greatly improved. When you first came to court, your rusticity was dreadful; but now the case is changed.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Yes, then I was in love with cousin Deborah.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: True, and a queer sort of love that was; for it made you look awkward and babyish—made you sigh and moan— write sonnets to the moon—and cry, “Oh Deborah! Deborah! Deb!”

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: But now—

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Now, you love as a man of fashion ought; now, you know what real Court love is. That love which makes a man gay—dissipates the wrinkles of care—dresses his face with smiles—animates his frame with grace elastic—and tips his tongue with the essential oil of wit.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: (claps his hands) There there! that’s it. Oh, if I could only talk as well as that.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Live in hopes, my pupil—live in hopes. If you improve as much during the next year, as you have during the past, you will chance to become (aside) as worthless a fellow as myself. But, tell me have you got a new sweetheart?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Oh, yes. Such a lovely girl—so young—so interesting.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: What’s her name?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Flora.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: (taking out memorandum book and pencil) Where is she to be found?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Oh—she—(aside) Stop! let me consider. I have been told that his grace employs his friends like jackalls to hunt out beauty for him; I must be cautious here—I won’t lose Flora. Where is she to be found did you ask?

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Exactly so. Where is she to be found?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: And pray, my lord, if I may be so bold, why do you want to know?

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Hey—hem—hey! Oh, for no particular reason— simply for the sake of conversation—nothing more. But, pray, sir, if this Mistress Flora is pretty, and fit to be seen— why do you refuse to tell me where she is to be found?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Hey—oh—why really—I refused simply for the sake of conversation—nothing more.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: (takes off hat and bows) Master Lilac Lovel, I am in your debt.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: I am most sorry to hear it, my lord—for people do say that you are not in the habit of paying.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: But this is a debt of honour, and must be attended to. But see, here comes my trusty agent. Come hither, good Mirondelle, and deliver your message.

div.stage: Enter MIRONDELLE, R.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Good even to your lordships.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: I am not a lord, my friend.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: I know it, my lord! but when I speak to a court gallant, I always say “my lord!” because it pleases them, my lord; it’s part of my policy, my lord! that’s the reason I called you a lord—my lord.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: You are a shrewd fellow, I perceive.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: You are right, he is so. In fact, I think there is only one in all the world more so.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: And that of course is yourself.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Oh, really—consider my modesty. What’s the news, Mirondelle?

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Am I to speak before this young lord?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: I will retire.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: By no means. Yes, sirrah! speak on—we’ll teach this young gallant how to carry on an amour, Buckingham fashion.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Then, my lord, know that I have discovered the abode of that woman, whom your grace admired so much at church last Sunday.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: What! the lovely creature in the shabby blue mantle ?

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: (R.) The same.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Excellent friend! You are my best Mercury! This gold piece as a reward. (feels in his pocket, then turns to LILAC) Lilac, have you a gold piece you will lend me?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Here is one, my lord. (gives one)

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Thank you, Lilac, I am in your debt. Take this gold, Mirondelle—as a reward. (gives it) The charms of that woman have fascinated me.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: She is very poor.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: So much the better—being pretty too.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: She lives in Eton.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Eton?—the very place where Flora’s parents live.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Hey—did you say anything?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Nothing particular, my lord.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Hem! I wish people wouldn’t talk to themselves. Go on, Mirondelle.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: I have seen and spoken to her, and arranged that your lordship shall visit her this evening.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Admirable Mirondelle !

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: In the capacity of a friend, to relieve her wants; of course your grace will improve the opportunity.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Of course my grace will

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: I will take care that her husband is out of the way.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Surpassing Mirondelle !

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Your lordship may consider the game certain—I will conduct you to the house, myself.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Inimitable Mirondelle! you shall have a rich reward—another piece of gold upon the spot shall be yours. (feels in pockets) Lilac, will you lend me some more gold?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Can’t spare any more, my lord.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Oh, never mind, it does not matter at all—not at all. Mirondelle, I am in your debt, (flourish, L.) Oh, the king is coming; away, away. But first tell me the name of the lady.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Her name is Rosabelle!

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Enough; hence—away. Flourish.—Exit MIRONDELLE, R. Enter YEOMEN OF GUARD, PAGES, LADIES OF THE COURT, DUCHESS OF DEVONSHIRE, COUNTESS OF CASTLEMAINE, MISS JENNINGS, LADY STUART, WALLER, CHRYSTAL, COURTIERS, CLARENDON, BUTLER, and KING CHARLES, L, U, E. Health to your majesty!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: (C.) Ha, my merry Villiers I rejoice to see you—I am yours to the shoe tie.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: (R.) Your majesty, I am yours to the ground.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Nay, my lord, I am yours to the antipodes.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Sire! I am yours to the lowest pit of Pandemonium.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Indeed Then there having no fancy for the spot myself, I leave you, (turns away)

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: I am in his majesty’s debt.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: (gives paper to WALLER) Sir Poet, I have read these verses addressed to me by you. By the-bye, did you not write some of a similarly flattering description to Cromwell?

sp.speaker: Wall.
sp.p: (aside, L. C.) His majesty has a good memory. It is true, sire, I did so; but––

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Hold do not seek to excuse the action, I am not angry about it. I merely named it to remark that these verses eulogising us, are not so good as those in which you praised Cromwell,

sp.speaker: Wall.
sp.p: Sire, your majesty must remember that poets always write better of fiction than of truth,

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Go to go to! thou art ready witted—give me back the paper, we’ll read these said verses again. (takes paper) Lords and ladies, prithee proceed—I will join you at the banquet.

sp.speaker: Clar.
sp.p: R. C. May I crave your majesty’s attention? there are some state affairs which require looking to.

sp.speaker: Butler.
sp.p: L. C. And I, too, would most willingly have audience with your majesty. The discontents in Ireland are increasing.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: (to CLARENDON) My lord, we beg a truce for to-day. Your grace, (to BUTLER) we entreat your silence till to-morrow.

sp.speaker: Clar.
sp.p: Sire, it is my duty to speak.

sp.speaker: Butler.
sp.p: Your majesty must be told of this.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: How to silence these busy statesmen? I have it. (brings DUCHESS from L., and passes her to CLARENDON, R.) Lord Clarendon, the Duchess of Devonshire, whose charms all honour, solicits the support of your arm. (she takes his arm— brings down COUNTESS from R., and passes her to BUTLER, L.) Your grace, the bright-eyed Countess of Castlemaine supplicates you to become her escort. (she takes BUTLER’S arm— CLARENDON and BUTLER express vexation) Forward, friends, I will join you at the banquet.

sp.speaker: Countess.
sp.p: We shall lack your presence.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: But not for long—proceed.

div.stage: Flourish.—Exeunt CLARENDON and DUCHESS, BUTLER and COUNTESS, and all but SEDLEY, CHRYSTAL, BUCKINGHAM, LILAC, and CHARLES, R. U. E.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: (to LILAC, L.) She wasn’t in the garden.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Wasn’t she? Oh, then, perhaps I made a mistake— it’s very possible.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: I think it was a mistake made on purpose.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: (touching his sword) You may think what you please, sir, but be polite. CHRYSTAL crosses to R., LILAC following— CHARLES comes between them

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: What ho, Master Lovell! Stay you here.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: (L.) Sire, I obey.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: (to CHRYSTAL) You I do not need. CHRYSTAL bows, shakes his fist behind CHARLES at LILAC, and exits, R. U. E. Listen, gentlemen; this night, if it consort with your humour I would have the company of all of ye.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: L. C., aside To-night? How unlucky.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: R., aside To-night? How provoking.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: R. C. To-night, it is our intention in strict disguise to visit Eton.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Eton?

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Eton?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Aye, gentlemen—Eton. For there, as we have been informed, there lives a lovely woman.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: A lovely woman!

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: A lovely woman!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: A lovely woman.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: I hope it isn’t my Flora the angel.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: I’m afraid it’s my Rosabelle—the devil!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: You must accompany me.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Break my appointment with Flora?—Impossible !

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Break my appointment with Rosabelle? Impracticable!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: How now, gentles? You do not reply.

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: (L.) Your majesty’s goodness overpowers us.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: (to LILAC) And you—

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Your majesty’s commands are law to me.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: (to BUCK) And—

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Your majesty’s wishes are commands to me.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: I’ll miss him in the streets.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: I’ll lose him in the fog.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: I’m told she is young, beautiful, and poor.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: My Flora, for certain.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: My Rosa, poz!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: She is said to be very virtuous—and it is our intention to try a little scheme upon her, and see if for the sake of rigid virtue there is a woman who can resist the many temptations, both of menace and entreaty, with which a king is armed.

sp.speaker: All.
sp.p: (laugh) For the sake of virtue?

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: Your majesty is quite facetious.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Oh, quite so; but from experience, I have learnt that some extravagance is threatened when his majesty moralizes.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: For the sake of virtue? Really, your majesty—ha, ha, ha! to think such a thing was possible. To be sure I might have thought so myself, before I became his Grace’s pupil, but now I know better.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Nay, gentlemen, I think you under-rate the sex; I entertain no doubt I could find a hundred such.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: A hundred! your majesty overwhelms us; can you find one such?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: I think I can—aye, and by noon to-morrow.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: I’ll bet five hundred pounds you don’t.

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: And I will bet your majesty likewise.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: And so, sire, will I.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Done—done done, gentlemen, with ye all; but remember, you accompany me in the adventure of to-night; give me your arm, Sedley, and as we pass the terrace, I will tell you my plan—trust me, sirs, I shall win the wager.

div.stage: Flourish.—Exit with SEDLEY, R. U. E.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Your majesty must not be so certain. I think I shall win the wager.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: But, my lord, if—if—if we should lose?

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Why, then I shall be in his majesty’s debt. Exit, B. U. E.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: And I shall do an impossibility for I shall lose more than I ever possessed; but there’s no fear of losing; the only fear I have is that of losing my appointment with Flora, and to make sure of that, I shall lose the king in the streets of Eton. Then, as to Chrystal, if he is jealous—why, I wear a sword, and will give him the satisfaction of gentleman. Exit, R. U. E.

div.head: Scene Second.—
div.stage: Ancient Street of Eton—moonlight—stage half dark.
Enter TYBBE, followed by five WATCHMEN, R.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: It’s only constables who know the onerous duties of the important office. Here am I, Sampson Tybbe, tailor by trade, compelled to be out of my comfortable home almost every night—parading the streets, or else sitting in the watch-house, smoking and drinking; and all for the good of the town.

sp.speaker: 1st Watch.
sp.p: But, Master Tybbe—

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Silence; how dare you “but” in the presence of a constable.

sp.speaker: 1st Watch.
sp.p: I was only going to say—

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Silence! I won’t have any idle chattering.

sp.speaker: 1st Watch.
sp.p: Master Tybbe—here—

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Silence! Oh the important duties of a constable. —Here have I got to parade the streets—hear cases at the watch-house—quell all disturbances—lock up disorderlies— rebuke the noisy—keep the watchmen from going to sleep— eat three suppers and five breakfasts for the good of the public.

sp.speaker: 1st Watch.
sp.p: But master Tybbe ––

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Silence! You do nothing but chatter—chatter— chatter—chatter. It’s only a night-constable that knows what it is to keep watchmen from chattering: they’re as talkative as women—as proud as peacocks—as stupid as pigs, and as sleepy as dormice.

sp.speaker: 1st Watch.
sp.p: Really, I must say

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Silence! Oh dear!—dear!—what a wondrous deal has a constable to endure, to be sure. Up early and late —preserving order, and pickling offenders. Now, you men, go to your beats—and mind that you perform your duties with discretion—while I go to the watch-house, and eat my third supper, and take a gentle nap—all for the good of the town.

sp.speaker: 1st Watch.
sp.p: But there’s a great—

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Silence! Don’t talk so; a talking man is a little doer—think—think, instead of talking.

sp.speaker: 1st Watch.
sp.p: There’s a great row going on—

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Sil—Hey? — What?—A row?

sp.speaker: 1st Watch.
sp.p: Yes, there’s some drunken men a beating the watch next the bridge.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Then run –run to their assistance. You wretches to let your brothers be punished, and not to tell me of it before.

sp.speaker: 1st Watch.
sp.p: You wouldn’t let us speak—you –

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Silence! Be off to the spot where the row is, and do your duty as Englishmen and watchmen. Exeunt WATCHMEN, R. Oh, dear, dear, wonderful are the duties incumbent upon a constable of the night. Now, here’s a row broken out, and I shall have to examine the prisoners, and commit them, and have an extra glass of grog, and all for the good of the public. Music.—Exit, L.

div.stage: Enter MIRONDELLE and two RUFFIANS, R.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Now attend to your instructions. This man, the woman’s husband, must be kept safely locked up until morning, and then let him go about his business.

sp.speaker: 1st Ruffian.
sp.p: Then, there’s no necessity to cut his throat?

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Not the slightest! Those who cut throats sometimes get ropes put round their own in return.

sp.speaker: 1st Ruffian.
sp.p: Shall we go to the house and force him away?

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: By no means; he’s coming out in a minute; I heard him say so, as I was listening at the keyhole, and then you can seize him as he passes through this street. Is the cellar in which he is to be locked all ready for him?

sp.speaker: 1st Ruffian.
sp.p: It is. (looks, R.) Here’s somebody coming— Is this he?

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: No, no, this is some drunken sot, going from an ale-house.

sp.speaker: 1st Ruffian.
sp.p: I shall rob him?

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: No such thing; never shoot a tomtit when you can get a partridge, it’s bad policy.

div.stage: Enter PETER, R.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.l: Tol de rol! lol de rol!
How merry we live that are Cavaliers hight,
We laugh all the day, and we sing all the night.
sp.p: I like being a Cavalier a vast deal better than being a Puritan soldier—there’s more fun in it; when I was a corporal I got nothing but cavalier treatment; but now that I’m a Cavalier I get corporal comforts.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Why, Corporal Peter Praying-for-peace, is that you?

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p:
No, it’s not me, I’m beside myself; I’m another
man. I used to be a dreadful fellow of a Puritan soldier, but now I am a true and loyal cavalier soldier, and I’ll fight for my king as long as I can stand upon my legs. (falls)

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: (helping him up) You’ve quite changed your principles, I perceive—that’s good policy now.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: Principles be hanged—the times change and we change with them—that’s all! But, I say, my buck, who are you that ask all these questions? Why, it’s, as I live, it’s my comrade, Serjeant Mathew Smite-the-proud.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Hush! hush! don’t mention that name; I used to be a dreadful fellow of a Puritan, but now I’m a true and loyal subject.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: But, I say, you’ve been changing your principles, hey?

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Principles be hanged! The times change, and we change with them—that’s all! There, can you walk steady now?

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: Walk steady? of course I can; my tumbling down just now was next to nothing. I’ll tell you how it was. You see the flesh conquered the spirit with regard to drinking first, and then the spirit conquered the flesh with regard to falling down afterwards. But, I say, isn’t there a tavern anywhere about here, where we can have a tankard together?

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Yes, but I can’t go with you just at present—I’m busy!

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: Well, then, I’ll go and wait for you. Where’s the tavern?

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: (points, L.) Why, do you see that light?

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: I see two lights!

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Nonsense, there’s only one.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: Oh, I see the one you mean; it’s dancing and jigging about like mad.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Well, go there and wait for me.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: I will; but don’t be long, or I shall come back to look for you. sings Tel de rol!
sp.l: How merry we live who are Cavaliers hight,
We laugh all the day, and we joke all the night.

sp.speaker: 1st Ruffian.
sp.p: (looking off, R.) Here’s another coming, perhaps this is he.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: No, no; don’t I tell you he will come this way? (points) You don’t know your right hand from your left.

div.stage: Enter BUCKINGHAM, R., in disguise cloak and hat.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: I have given the king the slip, and left him to find his way as well as he can—I was in his debt, and now I think I have paid him.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Who goes there?

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Who are you that ask?

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: The watchman!

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: That’s a lie! It’s Mirondelle!

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Ah, my lord! I beg your lordship’s pardon.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: How goes the plot?

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Nothing could be better—That’s the cottage yonder, with the little garden before it (points L.) but you must be cautious at first, my lord, for the wench is said to be very honest!

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Honest—pshaw! Who ever heard of honest poverty?

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Back, my lord; back all of ye; here comes the husband. We’ll take him out of the way, and then the coast will be clear. (they retire)

div.stage: Enter PETER, L.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: That man isn’t come yet; I wonder if he’s here still! Music—they rush upon him—cover him with a cloak and bear him of R., MIRONDELLE then points L. to BUCKINGHAM, who exits L., and MIRONDELLE R.

div.stage: Enter CHARLES, R. 2 E., in cloak and slouch hat.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: A very pretty adventure truly—lost my purse, lost my companions, and lost my way. I never shall be able to discover the house of this lovely and virtuous fair one after all; confound it, how dark the night is! neither a star above the earth, nor a lamp on it. Buckingham, Sedley, and the page have all vanished, and egad! I’ve almost vanished myself, for the darkness is so great, I cannot see a yard before me. Lost, too, in a neighbourhood that does not bear the best of characters. Oh, Charles, Charles, thou art a fool for thy pains. (goes up)

div.stage: Enter VANE, L.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: My mind is now fixed. I will no longer contemplate with apathy the hunger of my wife—no longer listen with a breaking heart to the cries of my child for food. I will turn robber, and by the sacrifice of my honour at least earn a meal for them. (draws poignard) Ah, here is a solitary passenger— he shall furnish the means of prolonging life, of assuaging hunger’s piercing agony. (to CHARLES) Your money, or your life!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: (L.) Ah, who spoke?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Who art thou.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: A desperate man! One who hath suffered himself, and seen those he loves best do so, until the drear sight has made him reckless. Money, I say !

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Good friend, I would willingly serve you, but lack the means. I have lost my purse.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: That paltry evasion shall not serve you. Money, or you perish !

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: On the honour of a gentleman, I have none.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: Honour and gentleman are words which, to me, now hold no meaning, I would have food –food for my starving wife and child. Honour and gentleman are very pretty words, when a man hath a full purse, but very empty ones when the man dies for lack of bread. Money!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Nay, if it comes to this, although unarmed, I will not yield without a struggle. (a short struggle CHARLES wrests the dagger from VANE, and throws him, R.) Wretched man! in this base attempt, how fearfully have you failed.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: The time has been when I could have wrestled with the best man in England, but now I am faint—I have not tasted food since yester morn.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Great heavens! is it possible?

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: ’Tis true! but for myself I care not—it is for my wife!—my boy! It is their sufferings that make me ready to curse the hour the sun first shone upon me. But come, sir, (crosses to L.) to our home—ha! ha! Home I said, come to our hovel, see our misery, then judge for yourself—you hesitate? Nay, nay, you are safe now—the only weapon I can own, is now in your possession, no harm can befal you—come, this is the way; ’tis not a very merry sight I grant you, but yet it may be useful as a lesson, come! Exit VANE, L.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Yes, I will follow him—I will visit the abode of this miserable man, and if his tale of wretchedness be true, I will at least endeavour to assuage his sorrow. Exit CHARLES, L.

div.stage: Confused noise, enter MIRONDELLE, two RUFFIANS, and PETER, R.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: It’s disgraceful! it’s disgraceful! Master, whatever you call yourself, to treat an old friend in this manner, first of all you cover me up in a cloak and then you lock me up in a cellar.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: I’m very sorry!

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: Don’t talk to me about your sorrow, only think how sorry I am.

sp.speaker: Miron.
