sp.p: I’m not only sorry that I locked up you; but likewise that I didn’t lock up t’other man. It’s no part of my policy to make such mistakes as these.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: I do beg at least that you won’t do so with me again.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Ah, what do I see? why here comes one along the right road, and as I verily think from the very house. Yes, yes, this is our man: upon him, lads, when I give the signal.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: What, another fellow to be locked up in a coal-cellar? I’ll stay and see the fun, that will be some comfort.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Hush! here he is. (he retires up)

div.stage: Enter TYBBE, L.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Oh, the onerous duties of a night constable! nobody can ever fancy what they are, unless that great and important functionary chooses to mention them. Here have I been examining four prisoners, putting three into gaol, and kicking the other out—blowing up the watchmen, and blowing a cloud of tobacco—exercising my judgment, and my teeth. Yes, I’ve been labouring to get through all my business, and nine tankards of ale, and all for the good of the town.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: That’s him—now’s the time! Music—the RUFFIANS rush on TYBBE, put the cloak over, and carry him off, R.

div.head: SCENE THIRD.—
div.stage: Interior of a desolate Cottage in the outskirts of Eton; door in flat, R. C.; table with lamp and stool, C.; lights a little down.
ROSABELLE and her child EDWARD discovered.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Alas, how dearly am I suffering for one act of disobedience! Oh, father, father, little did I dream you could ever have thus deserted me! My boy, my poor boy, surely he would relent, if could he but behold thee, nor visit thy mother’s fault on thee.

sp.speaker: Edward.
sp.p: Mother!

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Hush, hush! speak not, speak not, my loved one, for I cannot bear to hear thy cries for food, and pass them by as though I heard them not. Oh, merciful heaven! was I born to see my child perish, and thus too?

sp.speaker: Edward.
sp.p: Where is my father?

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Gone to win gold, boy—gone to commit robbery, — perhaps murder, that—that—he may buy thee food.

sp.speaker: Edward.
sp.p: Mother, I am very hungry.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: I know it, I know it, child; but I have nothing to give you. Ha! I hear footsteps—’tis he, ’tis he!—thy father brings us bread. My husband.

div.stage: Enter BUCKINGHAM, and two RUFFIANS, door R. C.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Ha! who are these? (comes down, R.)

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: (comes down, L.) A great admirer of yours, fairest; one who loves a pretty woman dearly, and of all pretty women, you the most.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p:
Leave me, sir, whoever you be, leave me; carry these
soft words and flowing accents to the halls of luxury and opulence; in the abode of the starving they are strangely out of character.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: I come to relieve your wants.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: To feed my boy? Blessings on thee!

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: You shall have all the heart of woman can depict or desire, all the joys of life shall surround you, and I will be your slave!

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: This is either idle mockery, or scheming villany, I know not which; but yet I like thee not; hence leave me, sir, nor waste time so ungraciously as to spend it in insulting unprotected woman.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: You had best accept my offer—I am a powerful man, and a nobleman to boot!

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: A nobleman? I doubt it; it is not every one who bears a title that is a nobleman. I scorn you and your offers.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Indeed! then force must be resorted to.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Force? oh, my husband, where art thou?

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: (to RUFFIANS, L.) Seize this lady, and convey her with all possible respect to my palace in the Savoy. ROSABELLE screams Come lady, go quietly, and then the inter- ference of these men will not be needed. Music—BUCKINGHAM takes ROSABELLE’s hand, dragging her up, R.

div.stage: Enter VANE, door, R. C.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: How’s this? my wife!

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: (breaks from BUCKINGHAM, rushes to VANE) Save! protect me!

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Part them! (spoken through music). RUFFIANS seize VANE and drag him to L.—BUCKINGHAM again seizes ROSABELLE and forces her up, when CHARLES enters, door R. C.—Tableau.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Hold, hold, I say !

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: (releases ROSABELLE) Who art thou that issues forth commands with such an air of dignity—who art thou?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: R. C. One who is accustomed to have his commands obeyed !

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Perhaps so, master mechanic, in your workshop, surrounded by your menials, but here you are in better company.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: If I may judge by your actions, I should doubt that; an honest mechanic surrounded by his workmen is in much better company than lords in an outrage. I think I know my gentleman. I would speak with you, sir. (suddenly knocks off BUCKINGHAM’S hat)

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Villain! dare you strike me.?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: I beg your pardon! Ha! ha! ha! my Lord of Buckingham.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Known and jeered too by this knave—Take that, sir. (knocks off CHARLES’s hat) Hey? The king, by Jove!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: I hope, my lord, you are tolerably well this evening?

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Quite well, sire—I thank you.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Be kind enough to pick up my hat. BUCKINGHAM does so And now, your grace, bid these persons loose the man they hold.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: (R.) Certainly, sire; loose that gentleman, you rascals. RUFFIANS loose VANE and retire towards R. ROSABELLE goes to VANE, L.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Now that I look attentively at those friends of yours, Buckingham, they do not strike me as being of very gentlemanly appearance; rather more useful than ornamental, hey?

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Exactly so, sire!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Be good enough to bid them leave the chamber, will you?

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Most undoubtedly, sire—be off, you scoundrels ! RUFFIANS exit hastily, door, R. C.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: (to ROSABELLE, L. C.) Come hither, fair one. Oblige me, by making an apology to this lady!

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: With pleasure. Of course, madam, you will have perceived that this affair was nothing more than a jest; believe me, nothing more.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: (L.) The jest was worthy of the jester. The man who intentionally wounds a woman’s feelings, must be as destitute of principle as he is of heart!

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Sir, I am in your debt!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: And now pray, guide me from this place; but first, sir, your name?

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: My name is Herbert Vane.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Herbert Vane! Surely I have heard that name before. (aside) Ha! I remember! This is the very man who saved my life after the Worcester fight, and wedded my friend Lord Clayford’s daughter. So-so! and this fair dame?

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: Is my wife.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: The lord of Clayford’s daughter. But tell me why does not his lordship, who is wealthy, assist you?

sp.speaker: Vane.
Charles.
sp.p: Why not apply to the king?

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: I have done so—but vainly; the Duke of Buckingham is too good a courtier to allow the complaints of poor people to offend the ears of majesty.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: I am in this gentleman’s debt again.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: There is a good deal of truth in that remark. You know, my friend, (to BUCKINGHAM) I have often told you that I thought the king’s master of the horse was the greatest scamp in the world.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Exactly so; and I have repeatedly stated that I quite agreed with you—always excepting his majesty. (aside) I’ve paid the king one of my debts.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: But who are you who ask these questions?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Content you, for the present, with knowing that I am a friend. Take this ring, and when the clock of the college tolls ten to-morrow morn, hie thee to Windsor Castle, and there enquire for one Chrystal Joyce. For present wants (feels pockets)—my lord! (to BUCKINGHAM) have you gold?

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Only this, sir. (shews purse)

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: (takes it) Give it to me.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Your majesty is in my debt.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Take this purse. (gives it—he then aside to VANE returns dagger—VANE starts) And so till the morning farewell. (getting to door, R. C., with BUCKINGHAM)

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Generous stranger! words cannot speak our gratitude—but oh! when death stands threatening beside your pillow, may the remembrance of those you have saved hover like angels’ thoughts around your couch, and soothe your anguish. (tableau—closed in)

div.head: Scene Fourth. —
div.stage: The town of Eton, near the bridge, dark.
Enter MIRONDELLE and PETER, R.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Was’nt that brandy good?

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: Capital—It’s filled me with spirit.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: I say, how does that fellow seem to like his cellar?

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: Oh, as much as I did and that’s not at all! he’d have liked a little of my brandy, only I couldn’t spare any of it.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Ha! ha! I dare say the duke has found his wife complying.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: I found the husband lying when I peeped in at him —yes, there he lay on the broad of his back; and all he kept saying was Tybbe! Tybbe! Tybbe!

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Tybbe! what did he mean by that?

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: I don’t know; I didn’t happen to ask him—but I supposed that was his name; for some times he’d vary the cry a little bit, and then it was—“Oh, Sampson Tybbe! oh, Sampson Tybbe! where the devil are you, Sampson Tybbe!”

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Oh, that’s not his name; his name is Vane, or something like it.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: Is it? Well then all I can say is that it’s very odd he should call himself Tybbe.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: See—who are these people?

div.stage: Enter two WATCHMEN, L.

sp.speaker: 1st Watch.
sp.p: I can’t think where he is –I haven’t seen him this last hour, and he’s wanted at the watch-house, it really is very odd what can have become of Master Sampson Tybbe.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: Tybbe; do you hear that?

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Ah! I do indeed. Who do you enquire for, good friends?

sp.speaker: 1st Watch.
sp.p: Master Sampson Tybbe, the night constable, he has been missing an hour past.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: I’ve got the wrong man again, (aside) Indeed!

sp.speaker: 1st Watch.
sp.p: Yes, and it’s very odd he neglects his duty thus. WATCHMEN cross and exeunt, R.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: You wouldn’t think so if you knew as much as I do. Come along, Peter, we must go and release the night constable—oh, dear! how angry the duke will be—I’ve not acted in this affair with my usual dexterity at all.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: (without, R.) Here I am, a murdered constable. Help! help! I charge you—help, in the king’s name.

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: Hollo! why—that’s his voice.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Why, how the devil did he get out?

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: Oh, dear! I’ve just remembered that the last time I peeped at him, I forgot to bolt the door again,

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: That was bad policy.

div.stage: Enter TYBBE, R., followed by several WATCHMΕΝ,

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Come along—come along—nobody knows what a deal a constable of the night suffers for the good of the public.

sp.speaker: 1st Watch.
sp.p: Well, really you have ––

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Silence! I’m a martyr’d individual!

sp.speaker: 1st Watch.
sp.p: I was about to observe—

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Silence! I’ve been cruelly used– when I get home, I shall cut myself out a suit of mourning; here, have I been seized upon in the public streets—cruelly gagged, and carried into a coal-cellar—and then contumaciously locked up—and all for the sake of the public, but only let me see the wretches that did it, that’s all.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Let’s be off, (going L., turns round as if prevented going that way—runs off with PETER, R. 2 E., all the WATCHMEN after them, springing their rattles) There they are—seize ’em in the king’s name. Exeunt after them.

div.stage: Enter CHARLES, L. U. E.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Yes that still beautiful woman shall be the subject of my scheme; and I am greatly mistaken if she wins not my wager for me. (rattle heard, R.) What means this tumult?

div.stage: Enter MIRONDELLE and PETER hastily, R., cross and exeunt L.. Enter WATCHMAN and TYBBE, four WATCHMEN seize CHARLES.

sp.speaker: 1st Watch.
sp.p: Here he is – I’ve got him.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Hold him tight.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: What means this. Villains! unhand me! You shall repent this—you shall find that—

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Silence! I arrest you in the king’s name. You’re a well known bad character—you’re in the habit of locking up night constables in cellars—you wretch, you are. Bring him along.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Scoundrels, let me go. (carried off by four WATCHMEN, struggling, R., re-enter PETER and MIRONDELLE laughing, L. 2 .)

sp.speaker: Peter.
sp.p: Hal ha! ha!—how nicely we’ve escaped.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Ha! ha! ha!—yes, and some poor devil gone to quod instead of us. (a little up, L.)

div.stage: Enter BUCKINGHAM and SEDLEY, arm and arm, L.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: ’Twas odd enough, Sir Charles, I should meet with you.

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: Odd indeed, your grace? but what have you done with the king?

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: I’faith that’s more than I can tell you he was close by here a minute back. Odds fish, Master Mirondelle! is that you? you have but badly managed the affair to-night methinks.

sp.speaker: Miron.
sp.p: Don’t name it, my lord; I’m quite ashamed of my own want of policy.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Considering how the affair has ended—I do not imagine myself to be in your debt.

div.stage: Enter LILAC, hastily, R. 2 E.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Oh, dear! dear! what’s to be done—what’s to be done. Ah! my lord Buckingham, it greatly amazes me to see you standing idly here.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: And why so, most worthy pupil?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Why, have you not heard the news?

sp.speaker: All.
sp.p: No—no, what is’t—what is’t?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Well, only listen! They’ve taken the king to the watch-house.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: They have! Then we must rescue him.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: No time must be lost—the watch-house door is not yet closed.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Then we’ll storm the fortress. Away Mirondelle— collect your men. Exit MIRONDELLE, L. U. E. Follow me, lads—let the war cry be the watch-house or victory.

sp.speaker: Lilac, Sedley and Peter.
sp.p: The watch-house or victory! Exeunt, R., except PETER, going R., hears rattle, turns and runs hastily off, L

div.stage: Re-enter MIRONDELLE and RUFFIANS, L. U. E., cross, and exit, R.—rattles going—four WATCHMEN drag on CHARLES, R. to C.—SEDLEY enters R. to his assistance, BUCKINGHAM enters, R., fighting two WATCHMEN, get to L.—MIRONDELLE, two RUFFIANS, and WATCHMAN, fighting. TYBBE overcome, R. LILAC kicking him. CHARLES, BUCKINGHAM and LILAC have got the rattles—and springing them till the drop descends

div.head: Scene I
div.stage: Gardens attached to Windsor Castle, with statues; one in L. C. on pedestal, representing a shepherd with a crook– painted to represent life with face turned towards L. and practicable; a vacant pedestal, R. C.
Enter LILAC, R.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: That was a sad affair last night; sad in more senses than one: In the first place I lost my appointment with Flora, and in the second place I got a good sound drubbing. I'm no match for those Eton mechanics–my. elegant frame and polite demeanour are of no use whatever before their hard hands and muscular arms; now, if they had been gentlemen, and could have used gentlemanly weapons of war; (touches sword) I'd have talked to them with a pointed tongue. Oh, how disappointed poor Flora must have been at my not meeting her last night–poor little girl; I must really try and manage to meet her to night–if it be only to make amends for, her disappointment; well, after all, I love Flora–yes, that I do, and if it was not out of fashion to marry I'd wed her; but as the Duke of Buckingham justly remarks:–

quote.l: Love, light as air, at sight of human ties,
Spreads his soft wings, and in a moment flies.

sp.p: Hollo! there she comes–I'll hide behind this statue, and find out what it is that brings her here; but that's easy to guess– 'tis to see me. (he hides behind statue, c.)

div.stage: Enter FLORA, L.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: I am very glad I did not keep my appointment last night–for the vanity of Lilac is becoming quite disagreeable.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: I wish I could make out what she's saying.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: And then his threat about killing himself–now I come to think of it, it was very ridiculous.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Can't hear a word–I'll venture a little nearer.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: I wonder whether the person I wish to see will come here or not?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: That's me!

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: I really think, after all, I love him.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: I could have told you that.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: There is something pleasing about him.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: I should think there was indeed.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: But perhaps his greatest fault is his exceeding bashfulness.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Bashfulness! I'm amazed! Well–well, Miss Flora, if you think me bashful now, I'll take good care and make you change your opinion the next time we're alone.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: But that will wear off in time–and is really almost beautiful, when compared with the impudent audacity of others.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: How highly she thinks of me.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: Now, that Lilac Lovell for example!

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Why, it's not me.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: His effrontery becomes quite disgusting.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Oh, the devil!

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: And if he thinks women are to be caught by unblushing audacity he is grievously mistaken with regard to the female heart. Ah! I hear somebody approaching, (LILAC goes behind statue c.) it is he sure enough.

div.stage: Enter CHRYSTAL, R.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Oh, good gracious! It's that cur, Chrystal.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Good morrow, pretty mistress Flora.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: Good morrow, Chrystal.

div.stage: (a pause)

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: I wish I dare speak to her.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: What a pity it is, he's so modest.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: It's–it's a nice morning, miss.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: Ye–es. Are you afraid that I shall bite you, Chrystal, that you stand so far off?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: She wants him to go to her.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Yes, miss–I mean no, miss.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: It looks very like it, methinks.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: He doesn't move he doesn't take the hint; there's a snipe for you.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: I've a good mind to tell her that I love her. There's temerity.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: I wish I could persuade the boy to talk, but he won't –there's timidity

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Prefer such goose to me–there's taste.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Well, here goes, at any rate. Miss Flora.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: He's going to say something at last. Yes, Chrystal.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Do you know that–that –(a bell rings)

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: Oh, dear, dear! there's the bell–and I must go and see if I am wanted; it's twenty to one that I am not, in which case I will return directly.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: And shall I wait here?

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: Yes, dear Chrystal–and when I come back you'll tell me what you were going to say.

div.stage: Exit FLORA, L.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Dear Chrystal! I'm sure she said dear Chrystal; and if she did say so, why then I can't believe that she loves Master Lilac half so well as he has the vanity to suppose.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Ahem –Sir–r–r!

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Well, sir–r–r–r!

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: You have injured me in the most distressing manner –you have seduced from me the affections of her I love. Prepare to atone for it.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: I deny the charge. I have seduced nobody–and if you allude to Mistress Flora, I can assure you, she thinks as little of you as I do.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: You are a villain, sir!

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: You are a villain to say so.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Will you resign Flora?

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: To you?–never.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Then I demand the satisfaction of a gentleman.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: With all my heart–come on. (they cross swords)

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Once more, I demand, will you resign Flora?

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Never.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Don't be hasty in your decision–take time to consider of the consequences. I ask again, will you resign Flora?

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: And I reply, no.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: You'll repent it, I'm a devil to fight; will you resign her?

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: No.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: You will not?`

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: I will not.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Then I will. (sheaths sword) She's not worth fighting for.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: So–so; this is your resolve, is it? well, I'm satisfied, (sheathes sword) and yonder comes dear Flora, returning to me. I'll hasten to meet her. Oh, you're a devil to fight–ha, ha, ha!

div.stage: Exit, L.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p:
I'll be revenged!–but how? The little wretch is such a coward, he won't fight. Let's see what the Duke of Buckingham says upon the subject. (takes out book) "If a man thwart you in your love, never fail to punish him for it; this is due both to yourself for the loss you have met with in the affections
of the lady, and due to the lady for the loss she has met with in losing you," Yes, yes, all this is very true–I should like to punish him amazingly; but how is it to be done? that's the point at issue.

div.stage: Enter TYBBE, R.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: I've lost myself among all these grand places–and I've a strong presentiment that if I am found wandering about here, I shall be thought guilty of trespass. I wish I could find the right way; the warder said straight before my nose, and I've followed my nose, but if, as my wife says, my nose is crooked, why that accounts for my mistaking the road. Oh, dear, dear, there's nobody knows what a night constable has to go through–here, he has to go wandering about gardens and castles, committing trespasses, and all sorts of things, and all for the good of the public. There's a young gentleman, I'll accost him–Sir!

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Yes, sir. What can this funny old fellow want here?

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Can you tell me which way I must take to get to the king?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: And pray, sir, what may be your business with the king?

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Why, I wish to lay before his majesty particulars of a most horrible atrocity committed last night in the town of Eton, when a number of drunken men, first of all locked the representative of the king–that's me! up in a coal–cellar, and afterwards proceeded to thrash the watchmen of the town in a manner truly alarming and gigantic. There was one young man who fought in a surprising manner; he wore a black velvet hat, and a blue feather–I should know that directly.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Indeed! (takes off his hat, and holds it behind him)

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Yes, I should know him for certain, for he gave me a most audacious kick–and (looks at LILAC) and egad! you are not unlike him.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Really, you flatter me.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: That toe–I could almost swear to the toe; when 1 look at that toe it gives me such sensation; I declare if you had a black hat and a blue feather on, you'd be the picture of the fellow that kicked me–ah, and a most severe kick it was too; it was all over black and blue–at least, so my wife told me; but, as I justly say, there's nobody knows what a constable of the night has to go through–here he's obliged to quell a disturbance, and there he is obliged to make up a quarrel; now he's cuffed, now he's kicked–and all for the good of the public.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
Tybbe.
sp.p: Why not exactly; to tell you the truth, I want to see my daughter, whom I have not seen for nearly a week.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Indeed! and is she here?

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Yes, she is an attendant upon her gracious majesty.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Indeed! what's her name?

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Flora Tybbe.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Flora! Why this is Flora's father–now for revenge! (takes out handkerchief, and begins to weep) Oh, dear! oh, dear!

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: What's the matter.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: I'm weeping for you.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: For me! bless me, do I look very ill?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Unhappy–unfortunate Master Tybbe.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Oh, I feel so ill, all of a sudden. It's wonderful what a deal a night constable suffers –does my nose look blue?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: No, it's rather reddish.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Then I'm all right, that's its natural hue.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Your daughter, sir.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: My daughter, what of her?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: She's had the great misfortune to fall in love with one of the king's pages–a dissipated rake, and he's fallen in love with her.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: He shall never marry her–never. I've promised her to Simon Satin, the mercer of Reading, and he shall have her; she shall never marry this court gallant.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Why, I don't apprehend that there's much fear of that; you'll find he doesn't mean to marry her, he'll only bamboozle her, that's all.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: I'll thrash him to a jelly.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: That's right; thrash him to two jellies–see yonder, they come together.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: I'll hide somewhere, and listen to what they are saying, and if I find what you say is correct–let them beware of the night constable–Sampson Tybbe.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Yes; but where can you hide? This is the king's private garden, and nobody is allowed to wander through it without permission. Egad, 1 have it, you are dressed nearly the same as that painted statue and if mounted on this vacant pedestal–yes, yes, that's it. (gives crook to TYBBE) Here take this.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Well, and now I've got it, what am I to do with it?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p:
Mount this pedestal–make yourself look like the other figure; place yourself in the same attitude–quick–quick, here they come, and besides there may be other people see
you, in which case I will not answer for what will become of you, trespassing as you are in the king's garden.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Oh, dear! it's wonderful what a night constable has to go through. (gets on pedestal–stands awkwardly)

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Now strike a position.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Strike a physician–I shouldn't like to do it.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Strike position, I said –one like that statue I moved.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: I don't know how to strike position–I'll strike that page if you like.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Put yourself into a situation like that statue, I say.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Oh, I know. There!

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: Left leg a little forward, right leg a little backward, head turned a little more.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: My head is almost turned already.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: There, that's very good; now stand quite still– listen to what they say, and as soon as you have found out the treachery of this vile page and thrashed him properly, you had better take the earliest opportunity of escaping. I must now attend to my duties in the castle–so much for master Chrystal; I think I have pretty well punished the little cowardly wretch without degrading myself by fighting.

div.stage: Exit LILAC, R.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Here's a situation for a night constable! I wish I could squat down like a tailor, there I should be at home; but to be mounted thus, on a pedestal, imitating a statue, and looking like a shepherd–and all for the good of the country.

div.stage: Enter CHRYSTAL and FLORA, L.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: Well I'm so glad, Chrystal, that you summoned courage enough to tell me you loved me.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Cockatrice!

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: Who spoke?

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Did not you?

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: No.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Oh, it must have been fancy then. Do you know, Flora, I really love you dearly?

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: I can't stand this.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: What is it you can't stand, dear? (to CHRYSTAL)

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: I did not speak, dear.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: You did not; why then, who could it be?

div.stage: (they look round)

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: It was me!

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: Mercy! mercy! a ghost–a ghost!.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Mercy! mercy! a frightful ghost!

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: Here's a walking statue; help! help!

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Here's a stone man talking; help! help!

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Oh, you precious pair. Flora, you baggage! ain't you ashamed of yourself?

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: Why, it's pa. Why, pa!

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Pa!

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: Why, pa, how came you here?

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Never mind how I came here, but answer me this. Ain't you ashamed of your conduct, in falling in love with this young man?

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: La, no pa! there's no harm in it, is there?

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Harm! harm! indeed–would you attempt to deceive me–me, a night constable?–harm; I should think there was, he never dreams of marrying you, he only means to bamboozle you.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: It can't be? Chrystal dear, you don't mean to bamboozle me, do you?

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Oh, no; give me only your permission, and I'll marry her to–morrow.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Oh, that lying young rascal, who put me up for a statue.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: Now, father; what objections can you have?

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Why, there's Simon Satin.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: What, haven't yon heard of him? Why, he was married last week.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Married! there's another lying young rascal for you.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Come, my dear sir, give us your consent, and ––

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: Oh, gracious me! here's some of the Court coming –oh, father, if they see you here–

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: If they do, I know not what will become of you.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: The king is so particular about keeping this garden select. Oh, what will you do?

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: What shall I do? You're a pretty baggage to be at Court and not be able to help your father out of a scrape, the only thing I can do is to go back to my pedestal.

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: Quick–quick then.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Pray be quick–stand quite still, they will not notice you, and as soon as they have passed I will come and release you. (TYBBE mounts the pedestal in his former attitude) Come along, dear Flora, if your father will but consent–

sp.speaker: Flora.
sp.p: How happy we shall be! Exit with CHRYSTAL, L.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Here's a nice mess I've got into! Well, only let me once get safe away from court, and see if ever I come back again. What a night constable has to endure–and all for the good of the country !

div.stage: Enter BUCKINGHAM, R.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p:
So, so, his majesty has got some woman upon whom
he means to try this notable experiment; well, I'm satisfied the wager will be lost, and by my winning the five hundred pounds his majesty will be in my debt!

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: That's one of the fellows who beat me last night.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Hey? What did you say? (looks round) Nobody here? I thought somebody spoke–I suppose it was an echo, although I never heard one before here.

div.stage: Enter CHARLES, R.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Good morrow to your majesty.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Give you good morrow again, my lord.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: There's another on 'em!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Odd's fish! what do you mean?

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Mean, sire? I mean nothing!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Nothing? Then pray, why did you say–There's another on 'em?

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Your majesty, I perceive, is jesting; it was not I, but yourself, who uttered these words.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: I? On my honour, 'twas not though. Here, Sedley!

div.stage: Enter SEDLEY, R.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Give us good counsel in this matter.

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: And prithee, what is the subject of the consideration?

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: There's another on 'em!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: That's it, exactly how did you know?

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: Know what, sire?

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Why know that we were speaking of the words you have just uttered?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Aye: the words "There's another on 'em."

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: Sire, it was not I who uttered the words–they seemed to me to be spoken by some one behind us.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Odd's fish! if I find any spy upon our actions concealed within these gardens, I'll cut his legs off. (TYBBE fidgets, and when he sees CHARLES turning towards him, assumes attitude, but with face turned towards R.) Search about, gentlemen, search about.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: (they search) Well, this is some strange delusion of memory. (seeing TYBBE) I could have sworn this statue faced the other way. (BUCKINGHAM and SEDLEY come down) My lord of Buckingham, say, without looking, from memory, which way faces the statue of the shepherd behind us?

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Sire, certainly towards the left, I have often noticed it.

div.stage: (TYBBE changes his position for the correct one facing L.)

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: So say I; but we are wrong, look at it. (they turn)

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Well, sire, you see it is as I have said?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Odd's fish! there is witchcraft here! Gentlemen, I swear to you the statue faced the other way. (crosses to L.)

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Has his sacred majesty been tippling this morning?

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: Upon my honour, I don't know.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Upon my honour, I think he has!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: I see, gentlemen, you do not credit my assertion?

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: I credit everything your majesty says; but still I cannot understand how this stone statue could move, solid as you see it is. (strikes TYBBE's body with stick) Or, how these lifeless legs could skip like those of Harlequin.

div.stage: (strikes TYBBE'S legs. When they move away, TYBBE rubs his back and legs, and as they turn, resumes his position)

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: I have strange suspicions respecting that statue; but at least I have hit upon a mode to solve them– your grace of Buckingham, and you, Sir Charles, pray notice this. (produces small pistol) This is an improved pistol, lately presented to us by its maker, and with it, by dint of practice, we have acquired such singular dexterity that, ninety–nine times out a hundred, we hit the mark we aim it..

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: What's his majesty at now?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Now we will wager aught you like that from this distance, we will place a bullet in either eye of yonder shepherd.

div.stage: (TYBBE becomes uneasу)

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: I'll wager nothing more, sire, till the wager we have on hand be safe decided.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: As you will; then without a wager, merely to display our skill, we will perform the task. (presents pistol) Now which eye shall I hit?–right or left; or shall I touch the nose, or strike the month?

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.stage: (jumps down and falls on his knees)
sp.p: Mercy! mercy!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Ha! have we spies here? Is this a plot against our life?

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: A plot? die, fellow!

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: Wretch, prepare to perish!

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Mercy! mercy! I'm not a spy, I'm a night constable–mercy! if you dare to kill me, I'll take you up for brawling in the streets last night.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Hold, friends! let us understand the truth. Speak, fellow, who and what are you?

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: My name is Sampson Tybbe, and I'm a tailor by trade, and I'm night constable of Eton.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Why did you come here?

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p:
I came here to tell the king what a row you, and you, and you, kicked up last night at Eton, and how you seized
>me, locked me up in a cellar, let me out, kicked me, cuffed, thrashed me, and all for the good of the country.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Why did you personate that statue?

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Because I was afraid I was trespassing here, and wanted to avoid detection–Oh, here's a situation for a night constable!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: My lord, I think we had better bribe this man to secrecy? – What think you, Sedley?

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: I quite agree with your majesty.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: And so do I, if your majesty will be kind enough to bribe him, I shall esteem myself to be in your majesty's debt.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Harkye, sirrah! The punishment of being found in these gardens is very slight–it only consists of the loss of both ears, and being branded on the forehead!

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Goodness! how few people there are know what a night constable has to go through–lose my precious ears!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Listen; but a person found concealed in these gardens is presumed to be guilty of treason, and accordingly not only loses his ears, but his head as well.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: I'm melting away!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: But these gentlemen, and myself, are willing to show mercy to you upon one condition.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Name it, only name it, anything, anything: you may have a jolly row whenever you like. I'm night constable –and I'll wink at it.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: What does your majesty mean to require ?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: His silence respecting last night.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Ask him to throw that little girl of his into the bargain.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Odd's fish, friend; we must not make such a request, seeing that we are the natural protector of our female subjects.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: That's true; I acknowledge your majesty has a great many such subjects under your royal protection.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Mark me, Sampson Tybbe, never again mention the affair of last night, and this affair of the morning shall be overlooked.

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Agreed, agreed, I willingly consent; only let me know when you want to have a row–and–

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Begone! lose no time, lest I change my mind, and hang thee at once!

div.stage: Enter 1ST MESSENGER, R.

sp.speaker: 1st Messen.
sp.p: Sire, the persons you expect have arrived.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: 'Tis well; act as I have directed you. Come sirs , this way.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: I shall win the wager, sire?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: We have to see that; come to the castle.

div.stage: Exeunt Messenger, R., Charles, Buckingham, and Sedley, L. flourish

sp.speaker: Tybbe.
sp.p: Hang? I'm off – I'm off! I'll only just go and give my consent to my daughter's marriage with that spruce young fellow, and then I'll be off. Oh, the duties of a night constable are great indeed! Here I'm first to lose my ears, and then my head, and after all that, I'm to be hanged! and all for the good of the country! Exit, L.

div.head: Scene II.—A Chamber in the Castle.
div.stage: Enter CHRYSTAL, L.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Old master Tybbe has given his consent to the marriage, and I am as happy as a prince. Now let Lilac bluster as he pleases, and boast of his conquests as he likes—I am satisfied with one true, one sincere and affectionate heart.

div.stage: Enter BUCKINGHAM, L.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Where is she? where is this paragon of the world?

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Did your grace speak?

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: I did; but you appear too much engrossed with your own thoughts to heed my words. What are you thinking of, sir?

div.stage: Enter 1st MESSENGER, R.

sp.speaker: 1st Messen.
sp.p: Master Chrystal Joyce, a man accompanied by a woman and a child, desire to see you.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: A woman, and a child! Why, Chrystal, my demure lad, what hast thou been about—hey?

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: I am amazed!

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: More amazed than gratified I should think.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Who can they be?

sp.speaker: 1st Messen.
sp.p: They send you this ring, sir.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: This ring! admit them instantly. ’Tis the King’s private signet ring! Exit 1st MESSENGER, R.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Indeed! then I know who they are, and I’m off— They are people that I am indebted to. Good-bye, Chrystal, good-bye; take care of the lady, and rock the baby on your knee—Oh, pretty dear! hey diddle, diddle, the cat and the fiddle—Ha! ha! ha! Exit, R.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p:
I have his majesty’s commands which must be obeyed to the letter.
Enter 1st MESSENGER, conducting VANE, ROSABELLE and CHILD, R., and four YEOMEN OF THE GUARD. Is your name Herbert Vane?

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: It is, and I am here by the wish of him who wears the ring I sent you.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: He asked you to come?

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: He did, and he told me he was my friend, and bid me come hither at this hour that he might prove himself to be so.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Herbert, dear Herbert, I like not all this questioning — Let us return.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: This looks like treachery. and I like it not, yet must I obey my instructions. You have said your name is Herbert Vane, now observe me, you formerly held the rank of Colonel in the army of the Rebel Parliament, and whilst doing so, fought against the king at Worcester—did you not ?

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: Why that question?

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Vane, we are entrapped here your life is sought— I see the dreadful snare—let us begone!

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Hold! you stir not—guard the door! 1st MESSENGER and two YEOMEN stand in first entrance, R. Will you answer that question.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: I will! I did belong to the army of the Parliament, and whilst doing so, I fought against the king at Worcester. But why—why was this done? Because the king sought to stretch his power to tyranny, because not content with governing freemen, he sought to reign over slaves. I will not be a slave, and therefore I fought against the king at Worcester. But mark me, sir, now that the contest is over—now that the liberty of my country is secure, the king has not a firmer adherent than I am!

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: You have not proved it by your actions.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: I have proved it by my actions and that, not merely since Charles Stuart became monarch here, but long before! nay, when after that battle of which you have spoken, he fled to Normandy, mine was the hand that saved his life. that aided his escape, and this I speak not out of paltry love of boast, but as a proof of that I have averred.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: I know not how that may be; but this I do know, that I hold a warrant for your committal to the Tower, as charged with treason.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: No, no; I cannot believe the king would thus repay an act of mercy.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: ’Tis too true!

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Then come, Herbert, we will die together, and by our deaths of cold resignation, teach the imperious monarch how little the unhappy value life. Come, sir, lead us to the prison.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Lady, you must remain here.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Here?—I!—Why?

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Such are the king’s commands.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: You do not mean, sir, that I am to be taken to the Tower, whilst my wife remains within the castle at Windsor?

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Such are my instructions—you and the child go to the Tower.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: (screams) My child!

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: The Lady Rosabelle remains here.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: I see it all, fair sir, thou silken emissary of a noble monarch! May I crave one moment’s private converse with my wife, ere I give her up to your care?

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: (L.) You speak very sneeringly, methinks, sir; nevertheless, your request is granted. (goes up—VANE puts his arm round ROSABELLE’S waist and draws her forward)

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: (L.) I know it all.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: (R.) What means it?

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: Wife, I well remember that, years back, Charles ex- pressed great admiration of your beauty—that beauty time, and poverty, and sorrows have impaired; but enough still remains to excite his passions. I and your child are to be sent to the Tower, that you, defenceless, may fall an easy victim to the king.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Dishonour!—death sooner!

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: Death—death! that’s the word.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Is there no other way?

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: None—we are hemmed in, we cannot escape, in another minute they will tear us asunder for ever.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: You have a dagger?

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: I have.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Then kill me. Strike—strike to my heart! Death sooner than dishonour—strike!

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: (after a pause) I will. (raises dagger to strike— CHRYSTAL rushes between them)

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Hold! madman as you are—withdraw your murderous hand—my orders are imperative, and must be obeyed; you and the child go to the Tower, and the lady remains here; but observe me, sir, I know what ’tis to love, and loving, to fear for the loved one’s safety; and here I pledge myself, at every risk to preserve the honour of this lady.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p: (L.) Will you swear that?

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: (C.) I will.

sp.speaker: Vane.
sp.p:
Enough!—I thank you: I am now ready for my new dwelling place. crosses to R. Rosabelle, my poor Rosabelle, I know we are not to meet again in this world—and—“Tis very hard to part, and thus to—but no sobbing no sighs no tears—nothing but hate—hate of the tyrant!—scorn of the
faithless monarch! Detestation and misery be the doom of him who tears asunder hearts that love like ours.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Herbert, my husband!—my boy!—my boy! (faints in arms of MESSENGER)

sp.speaker: Edward.
sp.p: Mother, mother, do not leave me.

div.stage: MESSENGER bears off ROSABELLE, L., followed by two YEOMEN. VANE and EDWARD exeunt R., followed by two YEOMEN.

div.head: Scene Third.—
div.stage: A white curtain with ermine spots, the whole width and height of stage, to draw in C., behind the first grooves—behind which the state chamber.
Enter CHARLES in disguise cloak, and SEDLEY, L. U. E.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Have my orders been carefully obeyed?

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: Sire, they have!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Where is Buckingham?

sp.speaker: Sedley.
sp.p: Waiting your commands in the corridor.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: And where is Chrystal Joyce?

div.stage: Enter CHRYSTAL, B.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Here, your majesty!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Where is the lovely Rosabelle?

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: She waits without, sire!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: How bears she her husband’s misfortunes?

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: But ill, indeed; she is like one whom heavy sorrows bend to the earth.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Sir Charles, await our presence in yonder chamber; let some one summon the remainder of our suite. Chrystal Joyce, conduct the lady hither. Exit CHRYSTAL, R. and SEDLEY, L. Now, is this boasted virtue but a fable, or has it tangible existence? We’ll prove the point, and should the lady deign but to be kind, why, though our wagers may be lost, we shall gain a fresh sweetheart, and one moreover who may chance to prove a formidable rival to Castlemaine—she comes! (goes up)

div.stage: Enter CHRYSTAL, R. leading ROSABELLE.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: (L.) Courage, dear lady, you have friends here.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: (R.) Would I could think so; but, alas, the unfortunate have no friends.

sp.speaker: Chrys.
sp.p: Nay, nay, I am your friend; and, besides, you have another here.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: And who is that?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: (coming down R.) I am he!

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Ah, the generous stranger of last night! Yes, yes, there is hope then.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Chrystal, leave us.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Oh, no, no!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: (R.) What do you dread? You are safe here, I merely seek to deliver a message from the king; leave us. Exit CHRYSTAL, R. Listen, lady, Charles Stuart, however circumstances may appear against him, is not ungrateful, and remembers well the service your husband rendered him on the coast of Sussex.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: And repays it by sending him and my helpless innocent child to the Tower.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: That was the act of his ministers! I see, lady, you know nothing of Court life; the fact is, the king is but a puppet in the hands of his ministers, they pull the string and the puppet dances accordingly; but the worst of the matter is that should not the puppet’s dancing please the company, they pull it to pieces, or maybe tear its head off, while those who moved it escape scot free. Here, however, the king is powerful enough to rescind the action. Yes, lady Rosabelle, it is the intention of his majesty to release your husband from imprisonment, and in addition, as a proof of his gratitude, present him with the attainted barony and estates which formerly belonged to his father.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Am I dreaming? Do not jest with me!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: I do not jest; but there is one condition attached to this.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: A condition of what nature?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: The king still continues to be a slave to your charms, and trusts his conduct in this instance will be rewarded by you with something warmer than mere gratitude, or friendship.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: It is then as my husband thought; and my dishonour is to purchase his freedom! Now, sir, take my answer—hasten to his majesty, and tell him that the humble Rosabelle scorns offers such as those, and knows not which most to despise, the man who could harbour hopes so dishonourable, or the still meaner wretch who could carry the proposal.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Think well ere you decide! Remember none but the king can save your husband. He is accused of treason— the proofs are clear! – He will perish!

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: So let it be! Herbert Vane would scorn to live dishonoured! and so too would I!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Your child?

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: My child! what of him?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: By your denial you seal his fate!

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Ha! speak out! To the purpose what’s to be his doom?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: No matter that; suffice it – you never more will behold him!

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Man! torture me not thus! (kneels) Give, oh give me my child!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: You ask in vain! You know the only alternative.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: (rises) I only know to scorn it;

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Be wise in time; remember, kindness to aking is rarely unrewarded; he will make your husband a wealthy baron—your child shall possess his friendship.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: And I shall forfeit all that makes life valuable.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Not so; that will not befal you; your kindness to the king shall be so well concealed that not the most intimate of his followers shall ever guess at it. Your husband shall never know what sacrifice it was that saved him; you and the king alone will know the truth.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Tempter, you madden me! But my child?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: By heavens, she wavers! Your child shall be within your arms in less than an hour—Charles will give him a dukedom if you desire it. Come, fairest, be wise, and let me tell the king you have decided !

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: (screams hysterically) Yes, I have decided; destroy my husband—deprive me of my child for ever—torture my brain to madness. Do all these things—yet—yet, I will perish pure and innocent! (falls exhausted on her knees, L.)

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Farewell, then! I must despatch the warrant for the immediate execution of your husband.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: There’s some strange mistake in this—I’m sure there is—you cannot be the wretch you would appear? Nature has stamped nobility upon your brows, and she does rarely err— my husband executed?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Aye, unless you consent. (going, R.) Farewell!

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: My child !—my husband —Is he gone? Is this dread tempter gone?

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: No, I am here. Perhaps you change your mind.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: No—never,

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Then I leave you. Listen, lady—soon you will hear the cannon which announces your husband’s doom. Exit, R.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: He is gone; I have conquered; but any child—my husband—1 have condemned them to death! He said the cannon would announce their doom! Oh, let me not wither beneath the horrid sound! (three cannons discharged without) Herbert! save him—save him! Falls pause—BUCKINGHAM looks on, R. and LILAC, L.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: Master Lilac Lovell, have you got five hundred pounds to spare?

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: No, my lord; have you?

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: By the saints, no—people say I am witty—but I fear I have hardly wit enough to get out of this scrape.!!

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p:
I’m beginning to think, my lord, that your instructions are not always to be depended upon; woman is a nobler creature than you appear inclined to give her credit for being; yes, now that the loss of Flora, and the conduct of this fair
dame, has taught me the truth—I evermore will acknowledge woman to be, as she really is, the queen of nature.

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: More sentiment—pshaw!

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: (raising ROSABELLE) Revive, lady—revive! The king approaches.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: The king? oh, heavens! Hide me from him—let not his pernicious eye rest on the unfortunate whom he has destroyed. (crosses to L.)

div.stage: flourish the curtains are drawn, and CHARLES, seated, C., with all the LADIES and GENTLEMEN of the Court discovered ranged R. and L.—YEOMEN OF THE GUARD, at back—PAGE with diadem on cushion, R.—PAGEwith goblet on gold salver, L.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: (rises) Master Lilac Lovel, my treasurer, desires to receive from you the sum of five hundred pounds.

sp.speaker: Lilac.
sp.p: (L.) Sire! he shall have it. aside When I get it.

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: My lord of Buckingham—five hundred pounds?

sp.speaker: Buck.
sp.p: (R.) Sire! I am in your debt. (goes up)

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: And I am in the debt of this noble dame, who has taught me to feel how far inferior is gilded vice to ragged virtue!

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: L. C. The king! May I believe my eyes? kneels

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: (to CHRYSTAL, R.) Bring me the diadem of brilliants. CHRYSTAL takes it from PAGE, R., and kneeling, presents it on cushion Receive from me this sparkling circlet; (places it on her head) and, now hear all—while I divorce this lady from the traitor, Herbert Vane.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: Mercy, my liege! mercy!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: And thus bestow her on a worthy and right trusty nobleman. (takes her hand)

div.stage: Enter VANE and CHILD, R.

sp.speaker: Rosa.
sp.p: My child! my husband!

sp.speaker: Charles.
sp.p: Approach, my lord, and receive your noble wife— noble—not merely because she wears a diadem—but because that diadem is won by virtue and by honour!

div.stage: flourish, till curtain drops

stage.p: GUARDS. PAGES. LORDS. BUCK. R. GUARDS. LADIES. LORDS. PAGES. CHRYSTAL. LILAC. L. VANE. CHARLES. ROSA. CHILD.

set.p: Time of Representation of the Two Parts—1 Hour 35 Minutes.

back.trailer: Printed by Thomas Scott, Warwick Court, Holborn.
